title: Asymmetric Goal Drift in Coding Agents Under Value Conflict
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, Published as a workshop paper in Lifelong Agents @ ICLR 2026
summary: Agentic coding agents are increasingly deployed autonomously, at scale, and over long-context horizons. Throughout an agent's lifetime, it must navigate tensions between explicit instructions, learned values, and environmental pressures, often in contexts unseen during training. Prior work on model preferences, agent behavior under value tensions, and goal drift has relied on static, synthetic settings that do not capture the complexity of real-world environments. To this end, we introduce a framework built on OpenCode to orchestrate realistic, multi-step coding tasks to measure how agents violate explicit constraints in their system prompt over time with and without environmental pressure toward competing values. Using this framework, we demonstrate that GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, and Grok Code Fast 1 exhibit asymmetric drift: they are more likely to violate their system prompt when its constraint opposes strongly-held values like security and privacy. We find for the models and values tested that goal drift correlates with three compounding factors: value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context. However, even strongly-held values like privacy show non-zero violation rates under sustained environmental pressure. These findings reveal that shallow compliance checks are insufficient and that comment-based pressure can exploit model value hierarchies to override system prompt instructions. More broadly, our findings highlight a gap in current alignment approaches in ensuring that agentic systems appropriately balance explicit user constraints against broadly beneficial learned preferences under sustained environmental pressure.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how autonomous coding agents drift from their initial instructions under long, realistic coding tasks, especially when environmental pressures encourage other values like security and privacy.
AI.motivation: Existing work on model preferences and goal drift uses static, synthetic setups that fail to capture the complexity and temporal dynamics of real-world coding environments. There is a need to understand how agentic coding systems behave over time when faced with conflicts between explicit instructions, their internalized values, and ongoing environmental pressures.
AI.method: The authors build a framework on top of OpenCode to create realistic, multi-step coding tasks for autonomous agents. They systematically vary the presence or absence of environmental pressures that incentivize competing values and track when and how often agents violate constraints specified in their system prompts. They evaluate multiple models (GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, Grok Code Fast 1) and analyze drift as a function of value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context.
AI.result: The study finds that the tested models exhibit asymmetric goal drift: they are more likely to break system-prompt constraints when those constraints conflict with values the models strongly prioritize, such as security and privacy. Goal drift rates increase with stronger value alignment toward the pressured goal, higher adversarial pressure, and longer context accumulation. Even high-priority values like privacy are not perfectly respected under sustained opposing pressure, showing non-zero violation rates.
AI.conclusion: Shallow or one-off compliance checks are inadequate for ensuring reliable adherence to system-prompt constraints in long-horizon, agentic coding settings. Comment-based or contextual pressure can exploit a model’s value hierarchy to override explicit instructions. This exposes a weakness in current alignment techniques, which do not fully address how agents should trade off explicit user constraints against their learned, broadly-beneficial preferences in the presence of ongoing environmental pressure.

title: Build, Judge, Optimize: A Blueprint for Continuous Improvement of Multi-Agent Consumer Assistants
summary: Conversational shopping assistants (CSAs) represent a compelling application of agentic AI, but moving from prototype to production reveals two underexplored challenges: how to evaluate multi-turn interactions and how to optimize tightly coupled multi-agent systems. Grocery shopping further amplifies these difficulties, as user requests are often underspecified, highly preference-sensitive, and constrained by factors such as budget and inventory. In this paper, we present a practical blueprint for evaluating and optimizing conversational shopping assistants, illustrated through a production-scale AI grocery assistant. We introduce a multi-faceted evaluation rubric that decomposes end-to-end shopping quality into structured dimensions and develop a calibrated LLM-as-judge pipeline aligned with human annotations. Building on this evaluation foundation, we investigate two complementary prompt-optimization strategies based on a SOTA prompt-optimizer called GEPA (Shao et al., 2025): (1) Sub-agent GEPA, which optimizes individual agent nodes against localized rubrics, and (2) MAMuT (Multi-Agent Multi-Turn) GEPA (Herrera et al., 2026), a novel system-level approach that jointly optimizes prompts across agents using multi-turn simulation and trajectory-level scoring. We release rubric templates and evaluation design guidance to support practitioners building production CSAs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a practical framework to evaluate and optimize multi-agent conversational shopping assistants, with a focus on grocery use cases, using LLM-as-judge evaluations and advanced prompt optimization methods.
AI.motivation: Existing conversational shopping assistants face two underexplored problems when moving to production: (1) there is no robust way to evaluate end-to-end, multi-turn shopping interactions, and (2) it is hard to optimize complex, tightly coupled multi-agent systems driving these assistants, especially in challenging domains like grocery where requests are underspecified, preference-heavy, and constrained by budget and inventory.
AI.method: The authors deploy a production-scale AI grocery assistant and build a multi-faceted evaluation rubric that breaks down overall shopping quality into structured dimensions. They then implement a calibrated LLM-as-judge pipeline aligned with human annotators. On top of this evaluation infrastructure, they apply and extend a state-of-the-art prompt optimizer, GEPA, in two ways: (1) Sub-agent GEPA, which optimizes prompts for individual agents using localized rubrics, and (2) MAMuT GEPA, a new system-level method that optimizes prompts jointly across multiple agents using multi-turn simulations and trajectory-level scoring.
AI.result: They obtain an evaluation framework in which LLM-as-judge scores correlate with human judgments and demonstrate that both sub-agent and system-level prompt optimization can improve conversational shopping performance, especially in complex multi-turn scenarios. The MAMuT GEPA approach captures cross-agent dependencies and provides additional gains over independent sub-agent optimization.
AI.conclusion: A structured, rubric-based LLM-as-judge evaluation pipeline, coupled with both local and system-level prompt optimization strategies, provides a practical path to improving production conversational shopping assistants. The released rubrics and design guidance can help practitioners build and iterate on similar systems, particularly for complex domains like grocery shopping.

title: Mozi: Governed Autonomy for Drug Discovery LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents promise to unify scientific reasoning with computation, yet their deployment in high-stakes domains like drug discovery is bottlenecked by two critical barriers: unconstrained tool-use governance and poor long-horizon reliability. In dependency-heavy pharmaceutical pipelines, autonomous agents often drift into irreproducible trajectories, where early-stage hallucinations multiplicatively compound into downstream failures. To overcome this, we present Mozi, a dual-layer architecture that bridges the flexibility of generative AI with the deterministic rigor of computational biology. Layer A (Control Plane) establishes a governed supervisor--worker hierarchy that enforces role-based tool isolation, limits execution to constrained action spaces, and drives reflection-based replanning. Layer B (Workflow Plane) operationalizes canonical drug discovery stages -- from Target Identification to Lead Optimization -- as stateful, composable skill graphs. This layer integrates strict data contracts and strategic human-in-the-loop (HITL) checkpoints to safeguard scientific validity at high-uncertainty decision boundaries.
  Operating on the design principle of ``free-form reasoning for safe tasks, structured execution for long-horizon pipelines,'' Mozi provides built-in robustness mechanisms and trace-level audibility to completely mitigate error accumulation. We evaluate Mozi on PharmaBench, a curated benchmark for biomedical agents, demonstrating superior orchestration accuracy over existing baselines. Furthermore, through end-to-end therapeutic case studies, we demonstrate Mozi's ability to navigate massive chemical spaces, enforce stringent toxicity filters, and generate highly competitive in silico candidates, effectively transforming the LLM from a fragile conversationalist into a reliable, governed co-scientist.

AI.tldr: Mozi is a dual-layer, tool-augmented LLM architecture that makes drug discovery workflows more reliable, reproducible, and governable, reducing error accumulation over long pipelines.
AI.motivation: Existing tool-using LLM agents are unreliable for high-stakes, long-horizon scientific domains like drug discovery. They suffer from unconstrained tool use, lack of governance, and compounding hallucination errors across dependency-heavy pipelines, which makes their outputs irreproducible and unsafe for real-world pharmaceutical use.
AI.method: The authors design Mozi, a two-layer system for LLM agents. Layer A (Control Plane) sets up a governed supervisor–worker hierarchy with role-based tool isolation, constrained action spaces, and reflection-based replanning. Layer B (Workflow Plane) encodes end-to-end drug discovery as stateful, composable skill graphs (from Target Identification to Lead Optimization), with strict data contracts and human-in-the-loop checkpoints at high-uncertainty decisions. The system follows the principle of free-form reasoning for safe subtasks but structured execution for long pipelines, adding robustness and detailed traceability. They evaluate Mozi on PharmaBench, a benchmark for biomedical agents, and via end-to-end therapeutic case studies.
AI.result: On PharmaBench, Mozi achieves higher orchestration accuracy than existing baseline biomedical agents, indicating better governed tool use and workflow reliability. In realistic drug discovery case studies, Mozi can search large chemical spaces, apply strong toxicity constraints, and output competitive in silico drug candidates while maintaining safety and traceability.
AI.conclusion: Mozi’s dual-layer, governed architecture substantially improves the reliability, governance, and reproducibility of tool-augmented LLM agents in drug discovery. By combining controlled tool orchestration with structured workflow graphs and human checkpoints, it mitigates long-horizon error accumulation and enables LLMs to function as robust, governed co-scientists rather than fragile conversational systems.

title: MAGE: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Language Agents toward Strategic Exploration and Exploitation
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in learned tasks, yet they often struggle to adapt to non-stationary environments with feedback. While In-Context Learning and external memory offer some flexibility, they fail to internalize the adaptive ability required for long-term improvement. Meta-Reinforcement Learning (meta-RL) provides an alternative by embedding the learning process directly within the model. However, existing meta-RL approaches for LLMs focus primarily on exploration in single-agent settings, neglecting the strategic exploitation necessary for multi-agent environments. We propose MAGE, a meta-RL framework that empowers LLM agents for strategic exploration and exploitation. MAGE utilizes a multi-episode training regime where interaction histories and reflections are integrated into the context window. By using the final episode reward as the objective, MAGE incentivizes the agent to refine its strategy based on past experiences. We further combine population-based training with an agent-specific advantage normalization technique to enrich agent diversity and ensure stable learning. Experiment results show that MAGE outperforms existing baselines in both exploration and exploitation tasks. Furthermore, MAGE exhibits strong generalization to unseen opponents, suggesting it has internalized the ability for strategic exploration and exploitation. Code is available at https://github.com/Lu-Yang666/MAGE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAGE, a meta-RL framework that trains LLM agents to both explore and exploit strategically in multi-agent, non-stationary environments.
AI.motivation: LLM agents perform well on static, learned tasks but adapt poorly to changing, feedback-driven settings, especially in multi-agent scenarios where both exploration and exploitation are crucial. Existing solutions like in-context learning and external memory enable short-term flexibility but do not internalize long-term adaptive behavior. Meta-RL can embed learning into the model itself, yet current meta-RL work for LLMs largely targets exploration in single-agent settings and overlooks strategic exploitation and multi-agent dynamics.
AI.method: MAGE uses a meta-RL training framework organized over multiple episodes. Within each meta-episode, the agent interacts with an environment and other agents across several episodes, with interaction histories and self-reflections packed into the LLM’s context window. The objective is the reward obtained in the final episode, so the agent is pressured to use earlier episodes for strategic exploration and later ones for exploitation. To better handle multi-agent settings, MAGE employs population-based training to maintain a diverse set of agents and introduces agent-specific advantage normalization to stabilize learning and avoid interference among agents.
AI.result: Experiments show that MAGE performs better than existing baselines on tasks that require both exploration and exploitation, particularly in multi-agent contexts. It also demonstrates strong generalization when faced with previously unseen opponents, indicating that it has learned robust strategic behaviors rather than overfitting to specific training partners or environments.
AI.conclusion: MAGE successfully equips LLM agents with an internalized ability to perform strategic exploration and exploitation in non-stationary, multi-agent environments. By leveraging meta-RL over multi-episode interactions, plus population-based training and advantage normalization, it achieves superior performance and generalization compared to existing methods, suggesting a promising direction for building more adaptive, long-term learning LLM agents.

title: AriadneMem: Threading the Maze of Lifelong Memory for LLM Agents
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents require memory systems that remain accurate under fixed context budgets. However, existing systems struggle with two persistent challenges in long-term dialogue: (i) \textbf{disconnected evidence}, where multi-hop answers require linking facts distributed across time, and (ii) \textbf{state updates}, where evolving information (e.g., schedule changes) creates conflicts with older static logs. We propose AriadneMem, a structured memory system that addresses these failure modes via a decoupled two-phase pipeline. In the \textbf{offline construction phase}, AriadneMem employs \emph{entropy-aware gating} to filter noise and low-information message before LLM extraction and applies \emph{conflict-aware coarsening} to merge static duplicates while preserving state transitions as temporal edges. In the \textbf{online reasoning phase}, rather than relying on expensive iterative planning, AriadneMem executes \emph{algorithmic bridge discovery} to reconstruct missing logical paths between retrieved facts, followed by \emph{single-call topology-aware synthesis}. On LoCoMo experiments with GPT-4o, AriadneMem improves \textbf{Multi-Hop F1 by 15.2\%} and \textbf{Average F1 by 9.0\%} over strong baselines. Crucially, by offloading reasoning to the graph layer, AriadneMem reduces \textbf{total runtime by 77.8\%} using only \textbf{497} context tokens. The code is available at https://github.com/LLM-VLM-GSL/AriadneMem.

AI.tldr: AriadneMem is a structured, graph-based memory system for long-horizon LLM agents that improves multi-hop reasoning accuracy and efficiency under strict context limits.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents must operate with fixed, relatively small context windows while maintaining accurate memory over long interactions. Existing memory systems fail in two key scenarios: (1) disconnected evidence, where relevant facts are scattered across time and require multi-hop reasoning to connect, and (2) state updates, where new information (like schedule changes) conflicts with older stored data, leading to inconsistent answers. The paper aims to build a memory system that handles these challenges while remaining efficient and context-budget-friendly.
AI.method: The authors introduce AriadneMem, which uses a decoupled two-phase pipeline: (1) Offline construction phase: It applies entropy-aware gating to filter out noisy or low-information messages before LLM-based information extraction, then uses conflict-aware coarsening to merge redundant static information while explicitly modeling evolving facts as temporal edges in a graph, preserving state transitions. (2) Online reasoning phase: Instead of iterative multi-step planning with the LLM, it uses algorithmic bridge discovery over the memory graph to find missing logical paths between retrieved facts, followed by a single LLM call that performs topology-aware synthesis using only the selected subgraph, keeping token usage small.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark with GPT-4o, AriadneMem substantially improves performance versus strong baselines: Multi-Hop F1 increases by 15.2% and Average F1 by 9.0%. It also dramatically reduces computation cost, cutting total runtime by 77.8% and using only 497 context tokens per query.
AI.conclusion: Structured, graph-based memory with a clear separation between offline construction and online reasoning can mitigate key failure modes in long-term LLM dialogue, particularly disconnected evidence and conflicting state updates. By pushing more work into symbolic/algorithmic operations on a memory graph and using a single, topology-aware LLM call, AriadneMem achieves both higher multi-hop accuracy and significantly better efficiency under tight context budgets.

title: AI4S-SDS: A Neuro-Symbolic Solvent Design System via Sparse MCTS and Differentiable Physics Alignment
summary: Automated design of chemical formulations is a cornerstone of materials science, yet it requires navigating a high-dimensional combinatorial space involving discrete compositional choices and continuous geometric constraints. Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents face significant challenges in this setting, including context window limitations during long-horizon reasoning and path-dependent exploration that may lead to mode collapse. To address these issues, we introduce AI4S-SDS, a closed-loop neuro-symbolic framework that integrates multi-agent collaboration with a tailored Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine. We propose a Sparse State Storage mechanism with Dynamic Path Reconstruction, which decouples reasoning history from context length and enables arbitrarily deep exploration under fixed token budgets. To reduce local convergence and improve coverage, we implement a Global--Local Search Strategy: a memory-driven planning module adaptively reconfigures the search root based on historical feedback, while a Sibling-Aware Expansion mechanism promotes orthogonal exploration at the node level. Furthermore, we bridge symbolic reasoning and physical feasibility through a Differentiable Physics Engine, employing a hybrid normalized loss with sparsity-inducing regularization to optimize continuous mixing ratios under thermodynamic constraints. Empirical results show that AI4S-SDS achieves full validity under the adopted HSP-based physical constraints and substantially improves exploration diversity compared to baseline agents. In preliminary lithography experiments, the framework identifies a novel photoresist developer formulation that demonstrates competitive or superior performance relative to a commercial benchmark, highlighting the potential of diversity-driven neuro-symbolic search for scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AI4S-SDS, a neuro-symbolic multi-agent framework with tailored MCTS and differentiable physics to search chemical formulation space efficiently and diversely under physical constraints, discovering a promising new photoresist developer.
AI.motivation: Designing chemical formulations in materials science is hard because it involves a huge combinatorial design space with both discrete choices (components) and continuous parameters (ratios, geometries). Current LLM-based agents struggle with long-horizon planning due to context window limits and path-dependent, locally convergent search that misses diverse, high-quality solutions. The authors want a systematic, scalable method that can reason deeply, maintain diversity, and enforce physical feasibility to accelerate scientific discovery.
AI.method: They build AI4S-SDS, a closed-loop neuro-symbolic system combining multiple collaborating LLM agents with a customized Monte Carlo Tree Search. A Sparse State Storage with Dynamic Path Reconstruction stores only key states and reconstructs reasoning paths on demand, allowing deep search without blowing the token budget. A Global–Local Search Strategy uses a memory-driven planner to move the MCTS root based on past results for better global exploration, while Sibling-Aware Expansion encourages orthogonal, non-redundant branches locally. A Differentiable Physics Engine with a hybrid normalized loss and sparsity regularization encodes thermodynamic and HSP-based constraints, enabling gradient-based optimization of continuous mixing ratios linked to the symbolic search over formulations.
AI.result: AI4S-SDS achieves 100% validity under the adopted Hansen Solubility Parameter (HSP)-based physical constraints, and shows significantly higher exploration diversity compared with baseline LLM agents. In lithography case studies, it finds a previously unknown photoresist developer formulation whose experimental performance is competitive with or better than a commercial product.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that diversity-aware neuro-symbolic search, supported by efficient long-horizon reasoning and differentiable physics, can effectively navigate complex chemical formulation spaces. It not only respects physical constraints and improves search diversity but also yields experimentally validated, high-performance novel formulations, suggesting broad potential for accelerating scientific discovery in materials design.

title: One Bias After Another: Mechanistic Reward Shaping and Persistent Biases in Language Reward Models
summary: Reward Models (RMs) are crucial for online alignment of language models (LMs) with human preferences. However, RM-based preference-tuning is vulnerable to reward hacking, whereby LM policies learn undesirable behaviors from flawed RMs. By systematically measuring biases in five high-quality RMs, including the state-of-the-art, we find that issues persist despite prior work with respect to length, sycophancy, and overconfidence. We also discover new issues related to bias toward model-specific styles and answer-order. We categorize RM failures by complexity and propose a simple post-hoc intervention to mitigate low-complexity biases that arise from spurious correlations. Our proposed mechanistic reward shaping reduces targeted biases without degrading reward quality and while using minimal labeled data. The method is extensible to new biases, model-internal, and generalizes out-of-distribution.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and mitigates systematic biases in reward models used to align language models via preference tuning, proposing a simple post-hoc reward shaping method that reduces these biases without hurting overall reward quality.
AI.motivation: Reward models guide the behavior of language models during preference-based training, but flaws in these models can lead to reward hacking, where models exploit biases rather than truly aligning with human preferences. Existing work has not fully eliminated known biases such as length preference, sycophancy, and overconfidence, and there may be additional unrecognized biases that could compromise alignment and safety. A better understanding and mitigation of these biases is needed to make RM-based alignment more robust.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate five high-quality reward models, including the current state-of-the-art, to measure known biases (length, sycophancy, overconfidence) and search for new ones (such as bias towards particular model-specific writing styles and the order in which answers are presented). They categorize reward model failures by their complexity and target low-complexity, spurious-correlation-based biases. They then introduce a simple, post-hoc mechanistic reward shaping procedure that adjusts reward model outputs to correct these identified biases using a small amount of labeled data, and test its generalization and robustness, including out-of-distribution performance.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that substantial biases remain in state-of-the-art reward models, including previously documented ones and newly identified biases related to stylistic preferences and answer order. The proposed mechanistic reward shaping method effectively reduces these targeted, low-complexity biases while preserving overall reward quality. It requires minimal additional labeled data and shows robustness, including generalization to settings and data distributions beyond those used to design the intervention.
AI.conclusion: Reward models used for aligning language models still suffer from systematic biases that can facilitate reward hacking, even in state-of-the-art systems. By categorizing these biases and focusing on low-complexity, spurious-correlation failures, the authors demonstrate that simple post-hoc mechanistic reward shaping can substantially mitigate such biases without sacrificing performance. The approach is lightweight, extensible to new discovered biases and potentially internal model signals, and can generalize out-of-distribution, offering a practical path toward more reliable RM-based alignment.

title: RAGNav: A Retrieval-Augmented Topological Reasoning Framework for Multi-Goal Visual-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) is evolving from single-point pathfinding toward the more challenging Multi-Goal VLN. This task requires agents to accurately identify multiple entities while collaboratively reasoning over their spatial-physical constraints and sequential execution order. However, generic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigms often suffer from spatial hallucinations and planning drift when handling multi-object associations due to the lack of explicit spatial modeling.To address these challenges, we propose RAGNav, a framework that bridges the gap between semantic reasoning and physical structure. The core of RAGNav is a Dual-Basis Memory system, which integrates a low-level topological map for maintaining physical connectivity with a high-level semantic forest for hierarchical environment abstraction. Building on this representation, the framework introduces an anchor-guided conditional retrieval and a topological neighbor score propagation mechanism. This approach facilitates the rapid screening of candidate targets and the elimination of semantic noise, while performing semantic calibration by leveraging the physical associations inherent in the topological neighborhood.This mechanism significantly enhances the capability of inter-target reachability reasoning and the efficiency of sequential planning. Experimental results demonstrate that RAGNav achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in complex multi-goal navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RAGNav, a navigation framework that combines retrieval-augmented reasoning with explicit spatial modeling to tackle multi-goal vision-language navigation, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language Navigation approaches, particularly in the emerging Multi-Goal VLN setting, struggle with accurately handling multiple entities and their spatial-physical relationships. Generic RAG methods frequently suffer from spatial hallucinations and planning drift because they lack explicit representations of spatial structure and inter-target reachability. There is a need for a method that tightly couples semantic reasoning with the physical layout of the environment to support reliable multi-goal planning.
AI.method: RAGNav uses a Dual-Basis Memory that couples two complementary representations: (1) a low-level topological map capturing physical connectivity between locations, and (2) a high-level semantic forest providing a hierarchical abstraction of entities and regions. On top of this memory, it introduces anchor-guided conditional retrieval to quickly select candidate navigation targets and filter out irrelevant semantic content, and a topological neighbor score propagation mechanism that propagates scores over the connectivity graph to align semantic predictions with physical feasibility. This jointly performs semantic calibration using physical neighborhood relations and supports better multi-target reasoning and planning.
AI.result: In experiments on complex multi-goal vision-language navigation benchmarks, RAGNav outperforms prior methods, establishing new state-of-the-art results. The improvements are particularly notable in tasks that require reasoning about inter-target reachability and executing long-horizon, multi-step navigation plans.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly aligning semantic reasoning with physical structure through a Dual-Basis Memory and topology-aware retrieval and scoring, RAGNav substantially mitigates spatial hallucinations and planning drift in Multi-Goal VLN. The framework enhances inter-target reachability reasoning and sequential planning efficiency, leading to SOTA performance and demonstrating the importance of jointly modeling semantic and spatial information for advanced navigation tasks.

title: From Conflict to Consensus: Boosting Medical Reasoning via Multi-Round Agentic RAG
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit high reasoning capacity in medical question-answering, but their tendency to produce hallucinations and outdated knowledge poses critical risks in healthcare fields. While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mitigates these issues, existing methods rely on noisy token-level signals and lack the multi-round refinement required for complex reasoning. In the paper, we propose **MA-RAG** (**M**ulti-Round **A**gentic RAG), a framework that facilitates test-time scaling for complex medical reasoning by iteratively evolving both external evidence and internal reasoning history within an agentic refinement loop. At each round, the agent transforms semantic **conflict** among candidate responses into actionable queries to retrieve external evidence, while optimizing history reasoning traces to mitigate long-context degradation. MA-RAG extends the *self-consistency* principle by leveraging the lack of consistency as a proactive signal for multi-round agentic reasoning and retrieval, and mirrors a *boosting* mechanism that iteratively minimizes the residual error toward a stable, high-fidelity medical **consensus**. Extensive evaluations across 7 medical Q&A benchmarks show that MA-RAG consistently surpasses competitive inference-time scaling and RAG baselines, delivering **substantial +6.8 points** on average accuracy over the backbone model. Our code is available at [this url](https://github.com/NJU-RL/MA-RAG).

AI.tldr: MA-RAG is a multi-round, agent-based RAG framework that reduces hallucinations and outdated knowledge in medical QA by iteratively refining both retrieved evidence and reasoning traces, achieving significantly higher accuracy than standard LLM and RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform well on medical QA but are unsafe due to hallucinations, outdated knowledge, and difficulty with complex, multi-step reasoning. Standard RAG helps but typically uses a single retrieval step based on noisy token-level signals and lacks iterative refinement, which is inadequate for nuanced medical decision-making that requires aligning multiple pieces of evidence over several reasoning steps.
AI.method: MA-RAG introduces a multi-round, agentic RAG loop for test-time scaling of medical reasoning. In each round, the agent: (1) generates multiple candidate answers; (2) detects semantic conflicts among them; (3) converts these conflicts into targeted retrieval queries to obtain new external evidence; and (4) updates and compresses the internal reasoning history to avoid long-context degradation. The framework extends self-consistency by treating inconsistency not just as a voting mechanism but as an explicit signal to trigger further retrieval and reasoning. The iterative process resembles boosting, where each round focuses on correcting the remaining errors of previous rounds until a stable consensus is reached.
AI.result: On seven medical QA benchmarks, MA-RAG outperforms strong inference-time scaling and RAG baselines. It yields an average gain of about +6.8 accuracy points over the underlying backbone LLM, demonstrating more reliable and accurate medical reasoning across diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-round, agentic refinement of both retrieval and reasoning can substantially improve the safety and reliability of medical QA systems. By turning inconsistencies among candidate answers into targeted retrieval and iterative error correction, MA-RAG achieves more stable, high-fidelity medical consensus than conventional single-shot RAG or self-consistency approaches.

title: AgentSelect: Benchmark for Narrative Query-to-Agent Recommendation
summary: LLM agents are rapidly becoming the practical interface for task automation, yet the ecosystem lacks a principled way to choose among an exploding space of deployable configurations. Existing LLM leaderboards and tool/agent benchmarks evaluate components in isolation and remain fragmented across tasks, metrics, and candidate pools, leaving a critical research gap: there is little query-conditioned supervision for learning to recommend end-to-end agent configurations that couple a backbone model with a toolkit. We address this gap with AgentSelect, a benchmark that reframes agent selection as narrative query-to-agent recommendation over capability profiles and systematically converts heterogeneous evaluation artifacts into unified, positive-only interaction data. AgentSelectcomprises 111,179 queries, 107,721 deployable agents, and 251,103 interaction records aggregated from 40+ sources, spanning LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents. Our analyses reveal a regime shift from dense head reuse to long-tail, near one-off supervision, where popularity-based CF/GNN methods become fragile and content-aware capability matching is essential. We further show that Part~III synthesized compositional interactions are learnable, induce capability-sensitive behavior under controlled counterfactual edits, and improve coverage over realistic compositions; models trained on AgentSelect also transfer to a public agent marketplace (MuleRun), yielding consistent gains on an unseen catalog. Overall, AgentSelect provides the first unified data and evaluation infrastructure for agent recommendation, which establishes a reproducible foundation to study and accelerate the emerging agent ecosystem.

AI.tldr: AgentSelect is a large-scale benchmark and dataset for recommending the best LLM agent (model + tools) for a given user query, unifying diverse existing evaluations into a single infrastructure for agent recommendation research.
AI.motivation: There are many possible LLM agent configurations (different backbone models, toolkits, and compositions), but current leaderboards and benchmarks either evaluate pieces in isolation or are fragmented. Practitioners lack a principled way to choose which full agent to deploy for a specific query, and there is little data that directly supervises query-conditioned agent selection.
AI.method: The authors build AgentSelect, which reformulates the problem as recommending agents based on narrative queries and agent capability profiles. They systematically convert heterogeneous benchmarks and evaluation artifacts into unified, positive-only interaction data. The dataset covers LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents, with over 100k queries, 100k+ agents, and 250k+ interaction records from 40+ sources. They analyze agent popularity and supervision patterns, and test different recommendation approaches including collaborative filtering, GNN-based methods, and content-aware matching. They also construct and study synthesized compositional interactions (Part III) and test transfer to an external agent marketplace (MuleRun).
AI.result: Analyses show a shift from a regime where a few popular agents dominate supervision (dense head reuse) to one where there is a long tail of almost one-off agents, which makes popularity-based collaborative filtering and GNN approaches brittle. Content-aware capability matching methods perform better in this setting. The synthesized compositional interactions are shown to be learnable, produce capability-sensitive behavior under controlled counterfactual edits, and improve coverage over realistic agent compositions. Models trained on AgentSelect transfer effectively to an unseen catalog in the MuleRun marketplace, yielding consistent performance gains in recommending agents there.
AI.conclusion: AgentSelect offers the first unified dataset and evaluation framework specifically for LLM agent recommendation, enabling systematic study of how to map queries to effective end-to-end agents (model + tools). It highlights the importance of content-based capability modeling over popularity-based methods in a long-tail ecosystem and provides a reproducible foundation to accelerate research and practice in LLM agent selection.

title: SE-Search: Self-Evolving Search Agent via Memory and Dense Reward
summary: Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) reduces hallucinations and factual errors in large language models (LLMs) by conditioning generation on retrieved external knowledge. Recent search agents further cast RAG as an autonomous, multi-turn information-seeking process. However, existing methods often accumulate irrelevant or noisy documents and rely on sparse reinforcement learning signals. We propose \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{E}volving \textbf{Search}, a Self-Evolving Search agent that improves online search behavior through three components, memory purification, atomic query training, and dense rewards. SE-Search follows a \textit{Think-Search-Memorize} strategy that retains salient evidence while filtering irrelevant content. Atomic query training promotes shorter and more diverse queries, improving evidence acquisition. Dense rewards provide fine-grained feedback that speeds training. Experiments on single-hop and multi-hop question answering benchmarks show that \texttt{SE-Search-3B} outperforms strong baselines, yielding a $10.8$ point absolute improvement and a $33.8\%$ relative gain over Search-R1.\footnote{We will make the code and model weights publicly available upon acceptance.}

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SE-Search, a self-improving search agent for retrieval-augmented generation that better selects and uses evidence, leading to significantly improved QA performance over prior search-based methods.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation reduces hallucinations in LLMs by using external documents, but current search agents often gather noisy or irrelevant documents and depend on sparse reinforcement learning signals, which limits their effectiveness and learning speed. There is a need for search agents that can more reliably collect high-quality evidence and learn from richer feedback signals during online search.
AI.method: The authors propose SE-Search, a Self-Evolving Search agent based on a Think-Search-Memorize strategy. It uses: (1) memory purification to keep only salient, relevant evidence while filtering out irrelevant or noisy content; (2) atomic query training to encourage the agent to generate shorter, more focused, and diverse search queries that improve evidence acquisition; and (3) dense rewards that provide fine-grained reinforcement learning feedback at intermediate steps of the search process rather than only at the final outcome, thereby accelerating training and improving behavior.
AI.result: On both single-hop and multi-hop question answering benchmarks, the 3B-parameter SE-Search model outperforms strong baseline search agents. It achieves a 10.8-point absolute accuracy improvement and a 33.8% relative gain compared to the prior Search-R1 system, indicating more effective evidence retrieval and utilization in QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: SE-Search demonstrates that combining memory purification, atomic query training, and dense rewards within a Think-Search-Memorize framework leads to substantially better online search behavior for retrieval-augmented generation. This approach reduces noise accumulation, improves query quality, speeds up learning, and yields significantly higher QA performance than existing search-based baselines.

title: LifeBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Multi-Source Memory
comment: A total of 28 pages, 8 pages of main text, and 15 figures and tables
summary: Long-term memory is fundamental for personalized agents capable of accumulating knowledge, reasoning over user experiences, and adapting across time. However, existing memory benchmarks primarily target declarative memory, specifically semantic and episodic types, where all information is explicitly presented in dialogues. In contrast, real-world actions are also governed by non-declarative memory, including habitual and procedural types, and need to be inferred from diverse digital traces. To bridge this gap, we introduce Lifebench, which features densely connected, long-horizon event simulation. It pushes AI agents beyond simple recall, requiring the integration of declarative and non-declarative memory reasoning across diverse and temporally extended contexts. Building such a benchmark presents two key challenges: ensuring data quality and scalability. We maintain data quality by employing real-world priors, including anonymized social surveys, map APIs, and holiday-integrated calendars, thus enforcing fidelity, diversity and behavioral rationality within the dataset. Towards scalability, we draw inspiration from cognitive science and structure events according to their partonomic hierarchy; enabling efficient parallel generation while maintaining global coherence. Performance results show that top-tier, state-of-the-art memory systems reach just 55.2\% accuracy, highlighting the inherent difficulty of long-horizon retrieval and multi-source integration within our proposed benchmark. The dataset and data synthesis code are available at https://github.com/1754955896/LifeBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LifeBench, a long-horizon benchmark that evaluates AI agents’ ability to use both declarative and non-declarative long-term memory over realistic, densely connected life events, showing current systems perform poorly.
AI.motivation: Existing memory benchmarks for AI agents mainly test declarative memory (semantic and episodic), where all needed information is explicitly given in dialogue. Real human-like behavior also depends on non-declarative memory (habits, procedures) that must be inferred from heterogeneous digital traces. There is a need for a benchmark that reflects this richer spectrum of memory types and long-term, temporally extended reasoning in realistic environments.
AI.method: The authors design LifeBench as a simulated long-horizon event environment, with densely connected events that require integrating both declarative and non-declarative memory. They ensure data quality using real-world priors: anonymized social surveys, map APIs, and calendars that include holidays, to enforce realism, diversity, and rational behavior. For scalability, they structure events according to a partonomic hierarchy inspired by cognitive science, allowing parallel generation of event components while preserving overall coherence. They then evaluate state-of-the-art memory systems on this benchmark.
AI.result: On LifeBench, leading memory systems achieve only 55.2% accuracy, demonstrating that the benchmark is challenging and that current systems struggle with long-horizon retrieval and integrating information from multiple sources and memory types.
AI.conclusion: LifeBench is a difficult, realistic benchmark for evaluating long-term memory in AI agents, requiring joint use of declarative and non-declarative memory over long, complex event sequences. The relatively low performance of current state-of-the-art systems indicates substantial room for improvement in long-horizon memory retrieval, reasoning, and multi-source integration. The authors release the dataset and synthesis code to facilitate further research.

title: Fine-Tuning and Evaluating Conversational AI for Agricultural Advisory
summary: Large Language Models show promise for agricultural advisory, yet vanilla models exhibit unsupported recommendations, generic advice lacking specific, actionable detail, and communication styles misaligned with smallholder farmer needs. In high stakes agricultural contexts, where recommendation accuracy has direct consequences for farmer outcomes, these limitations pose challenges for responsible deployment. We present a hybrid LLM architecture that decouples factual retrieval from conversational delivery: supervised fine-tuning with LoRA on expert-curated GOLDEN FACTS (atomic, verified units of agricultural knowledge) optimizes fact recall, while a separate stitching layer transforms retrieved facts into culturally appropriate, safety-aware responses. Our evaluation framework, DG-EVAL, performs atomic fact verification (measuring recall, precision, and contradiction detection) against expert-curated ground truth rather than Wikipedia or retrieved documents. Experiments across multiple model configurations on crops and queries from Bihar, India show that fine-tuning on curated data substantially improves fact recall and F1, while maintaining high relevance. Using a fine-tuned smaller model achieves comparable or better factual quality at a fraction of the cost of frontier models. A stitching layer further improves safety subscores while maintaining high conversational quality. We release the farmerchat-prompts library to enable reproducible development of domain-specific agricultural AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid LLM system for agricultural advisory that separates fact retrieval from conversational response generation, improving factual accuracy, safety, and cost-efficiency for smallholder farmers.
AI.motivation: Vanilla LLMs used for agricultural advisory often provide hallucinated or unsupported recommendations, overly generic advice, and communication styles that do not match the needs and contexts of smallholder farmers. In high-stakes agricultural settings, inaccurate or unsafe recommendations can directly harm farmer livelihoods, making it critical to design systems that are both factually reliable and culturally appropriate. The authors aim to address these issues so that LLM-based advisory can be responsibly deployed in such contexts.
AI.method: The authors design a hybrid LLM architecture that decouples factual retrieval from conversational delivery. First, they perform supervised fine-tuning using LoRA on a curated dataset of GOLDEN FACTS, which are atomic, expert-verified units of agricultural knowledge. This step optimizes the model for accurate recall of these facts. Then they add a separate stitching layer that takes the retrieved facts and composes them into full responses tailored to local culture and safety constraints. They also introduce DG-EVAL, an evaluation framework that conducts atomic fact verification by comparing model outputs against expert-curated ground truth, measuring recall, precision, and contradiction detection, instead of relying on sources like Wikipedia or general retrieval corpora. Experiments are run across several model sizes and configurations on crop- and query-data from Bihar, India.
AI.result: Fine-tuning LLMs on the curated GOLDEN FACTS substantially increases factual recall and F1 scores while keeping the advice relevant to farmer queries. Smaller fine-tuned models achieve factual performance comparable to or better than significantly larger frontier models, offering similar quality at much lower cost. The stitching layer enhances safety-related evaluation subscores—such as reduction of harmful or risky advice—while preserving or improving conversational naturalness and appropriateness for smallholder farmers.
AI.conclusion: Separating factual retrieval from conversational generation and training on expert-curated atomic knowledge enables safer and more accurate LLM-based agricultural advisory. The hybrid architecture with a LoRA-fine-tuned fact module plus a stitching layer delivers high factual quality, strong safety characteristics, and cost-effective deployment, especially when using smaller models. The DG-EVAL framework provides a more rigorous way to measure factual correctness at the atomic fact level, and the released farmerchat-prompts library supports reproducible and extensible development of domain-specific agricultural AI systems.

title: Specification-Driven Generation and Evaluation of Discrete-Event World Models via the DEVS Formalism
summary: World models are essential for planning and evaluation in agentic systems, yet existing approaches lie at two extremes: hand-engineered simulators that offer consistency and reproducibility but are costly to adapt, and implicit neural models that are flexible but difficult to constrain, verify, and debug over long horizons. We seek a principled middle ground that combines the reliability of explicit simulators with the flexibility of learned models, allowing world models to be adapted during online execution. By targeting a broad class of environments whose dynamics are governed by the ordering, timing, and causality of discrete events, such as queueing and service operations, embodied task planning, and message-mediated multi-agent coordination, we advocate explicit, executable discrete-event world models synthesized directly from natural-language specifications. Our approach adopts the DEVS formalism and introduces a staged LLM-based generation pipeline that separates structural inference of component interactions from component-level event and timing logic. To evaluate generated models without a unique ground truth, simulators emit structured event traces that are validated against specification-derived temporal and semantic constraints, enabling reproducible verification and localized diagnostics. Together, these contributions produce world models that are consistent over long-horizon rollouts, verifiable from observable behavior, and efficient to synthesize on demand during online execution.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to automatically build explicit, executable discrete-event world simulators from natural language using LLMs, aiming to combine the reliability of hand-built simulators with the flexibility of learned models.
AI.motivation: Existing world models either rely on hand-engineered simulators, which are reliable but hard to adapt, or on implicit neural models, which are flexible but hard to constrain, verify, and debug, especially over long time horizons. There is a need for a middle-ground approach that supports planning and evaluation in agentic systems while being adaptable during online execution and amenable to verification.
AI.method: The authors target environments governed by discrete events (ordering, timing, causality) and adopt the DEVS (Discrete Event System Specification) formalism. They design a staged LLM-based generation pipeline that first infers the structural interactions between components and then generates component-level event and timing logic. Generated simulators produce structured event traces, which are then automatically checked against temporal and semantic constraints derived from the original natural-language specification for verification and diagnostics.
AI.result: The generated world models can be efficiently synthesized on demand from natural-language descriptions and are capable of producing long-horizon rollouts whose event traces satisfy specification-derived constraints. The structured traces allow for systematic validation and localized debugging, even without an explicit ground-truth simulator for comparison.
AI.conclusion: By combining DEVS-based explicit modeling with LLM-driven synthesis and trace-level constraint checking, the approach yields world models that are reliable over long horizons, verifiable from observable behavior, and adaptable enough to be generated and refined during online execution, thus bridging the gap between hand-engineered simulators and opaque neural world models.

title: Language Model Goal Selection Differs from Humans' in an Open-Ended Task
summary: As large language models (LLMs) get integrated into human decision-making, they are increasingly choosing goals autonomously rather than only completing human-defined ones, assuming they will reflect human preferences. However, human-LLM similarity in goal selection remains largely untested. We directly assess the validity of LLMs as proxies for human goal selection in a controlled, open-ended learning task borrowed from cognitive science. Across four state-of-the-art models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Centaur), we find substantial divergence from human behavior. While people gradually explore and learn to achieve goals with diversity across individuals, most models exploit a single identified solution (reward hacking) or show surprisingly low performance, with distinct patterns across models and little variability across instances of the same model. Even Centaur, explicitly trained to emulate humans in experimental settings, poorly captures people's goal selection. Chain-of-thought reasoning and persona steering provide limited improvements. These findings highlight the uniqueness of human goal selection, cautioning against replacing it with current models in applications such as personal assistance, scientific discovery, and policy research.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models (LLMs) pick goals like humans do in an open-ended learning task and finds that they do not, warning against using current LLMs as stand-ins for human goal selection.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly embedded in workflows where they autonomously propose or choose goals (e.g., planning, research, policy analysis), many implicitly assume that their chosen goals approximate human preferences. Yet this assumption has not been rigorously tested in controlled settings where human goal selection is well characterized. The authors aim to fill this gap and evaluate whether LLMs can serve as valid proxies for human goal selection behavior.
AI.method: The authors adapt a controlled, open-ended learning task from cognitive science in which agents must explore, learn, and select goals over time. They compare human participants’ behavior with four advanced LLM systems (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5, and Centaur). They analyze exploration vs. exploitation, diversity of goals across individuals or instances, performance trajectories, and the impact of prompting strategies such as chain-of-thought reasoning and persona steering. Centaur, a model explicitly trained to mimic humans in experimental setups, is included as a targeted baseline.
AI.result: Humans gradually explore the task space, learn effective strategies, and display diverse goal choices across individuals. In contrast, most LLMs quickly latch onto a single high-reward solution and then exploit it (reward hacking), or they show generally low task performance. Different models display distinct but still non-human patterns; multiple runs of the same model show little variability, unlike humans. Centaur, despite being trained to emulate experimental human behavior, also fails to capture human-like goal selection patterns. Chain-of-thought prompts and persona-based steering deliver only modest and insufficient improvements in human-likeness.
AI.conclusion: Current state-of-the-art LLMs are poor proxies for human goal selection in controlled open-ended learning contexts. They lack the exploratory diversity, learning dynamics, and individual variability characteristic of humans, often engaging in reward hacking or underperforming. Hence, one should be cautious about delegating or substituting human goal selection to LLMs in domains such as personal assistance, scientific discovery, and policy-related research, and further work is needed to better model or preserve genuinely human goal-setting processes.

title: A Rubric-Supervised Critic from Sparse Real-World Outcomes
summary: Academic benchmarks for coding agents tend to reward autonomous task completion, measured by verifiable rewards such as unit-test success. In contrast, real-world coding agents operate with humans in the loop, where success signals are typically noisy, delayed, and sparse. How can we bridge this gap? In this paper, we propose a process to learn a "critic" model from sparse and noisy interaction data, which can then be used both as a reward model for either RL-based training or inference-time scaling. Specifically, we introduce Critic Rubrics, a rubric-based supervision framework with 24 behavioral features that can be derived from human-agent interaction traces alone. Using a semi-supervised objective, we can then jointly predict these rubrics and sparse human feedback (when present). In experiments, we demonstrate that, despite being trained primarily from trace-observable rubrics and sparse real-world outcome proxies, these critics improve best-of-N reranking on SWE-bench (Best@8 +15.9 over Random@8 over the rerankable subset of trajectories), enable early stopping (+17.7 with 83% fewer attempts), and support training-time data curation via critic-selected trajectories.

AI.tldr: They propose learning a critic model from noisy human–agent coding traces to serve as a reward model for training and inference-time reranking, using rubric-based supervision and sparse feedback.
AI.motivation: Existing coding benchmarks emphasize fully autonomous completion and clean test-based rewards, unlike real-world coding where humans are in the loop and feedback signals are sparse, noisy, and delayed. There is a need for a way to leverage these imperfect interaction traces to train better coding agents.
AI.method: Define Critic Rubrics: a set of 24 observable behavioral features extracted from human–agent interaction traces. Train a critic model with a semi-supervised objective that jointly predicts these rubric labels and any available sparse human feedback, using the critic as a reward model for RL or as a scoring function for inference-time best-of-N reranking and related tasks.
AI.result: Critics trained mostly from rubric-derived signals and sparse outcome proxies substantially improve performance on SWE-bench: best-of-8 reranking improves by 15.9 points relative to random reranking on the rerankable subset, critics enable early stopping with +17.7 improvement while using 83% fewer attempts, and they are effective for selecting good trajectories for training-time data curation.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-based critics can be learned from noisy, sparse real-world interaction traces and nonetheless provide useful reward signals that enhance inference-time selection, efficiency (via early stopping), and data curation for training coding agents, partially closing the gap between benchmark-style and real-world supervision.

title: PlugMem: A Task-Agnostic Plugin Memory Module for LLM Agents
summary: Long-term memory is essential for large language model (LLM) agents operating in complex environments, yet existing memory designs are either task-specific and non-transferable, or task-agnostic but less effective due to low task-relevance and context explosion from raw memory retrieval. We propose PlugMem, a task-agnostic plugin memory module that can be attached to arbitrary LLM agents without task-specific redesign. Motivated by the fact that decision-relevant information is concentrated as abstract knowledge rather than raw experience, we draw on cognitive science to structure episodic memories into a compact, extensible knowledge-centric memory graph that explicitly represents propositional and prescriptive knowledge. This representation enables efficient memory retrieval and reasoning over task-relevant knowledge, rather than verbose raw trajectories, and departs from other graph-based methods like GraphRAG by treating knowledge as the unit of memory access and organization instead of entities or text chunks. We evaluate PlugMem unchanged across three heterogeneous benchmarks (long-horizon conversational question answering, multi-hop knowledge retrieval, and web agent tasks). The results show that PlugMem consistently outperforms task-agnostic baselines and exceeds task-specific memory designs, while also achieving the highest information density under a unified information-theoretic analysis. Code and data are available at https://github.com/TIMAN-group/PlugMem.

AI.tldr: PlugMem is a task-agnostic, pluggable long-term memory module for LLM agents that organizes past episodes into a compact knowledge graph, enabling more relevant and efficient retrieval and reasoning than raw trajectory or chunk-based memories, and achieves strong, transferable performance across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory systems for LLM agents either need to be redesigned per task (task-specific, non-transferable) or are task-agnostic but ineffective because they retrieve low-relevance, verbose raw experience that causes context bloat and weak decision support. The authors aim to build a general, reusable memory component that works across tasks while keeping retrieval focused, compact, and decision-relevant.
AI.method: They introduce PlugMem, a plugin memory module that can be attached to arbitrary LLM agents without task-specific customization. Drawing on cognitive science distinctions between episodic and semantic/abstract knowledge, they convert episodic trajectories into a compact, extensible knowledge-centric memory graph. The nodes and edges explicitly represent propositional and prescriptive knowledge rather than raw text or low-level events. Memory retrieval and reasoning are then performed over this knowledge graph, treating knowledge items as the unit of access and organization, distinguishing PlugMem from other graph-based approaches like GraphRAG that typically use entities or text chunks as nodes.
AI.result: PlugMem is evaluated without modification on three heterogeneous benchmarks: (1) long-horizon conversational QA, (2) multi-hop knowledge retrieval, and (3) web agent tasks. Across all benchmarks, PlugMem consistently outperforms task-agnostic baselines and even surpasses specialized, task-specific memory designs. Under a unified information-theoretic analysis, PlugMem yields the highest information density, meaning it packs more task-relevant information per unit of retrieved context than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: A task-agnostic, knowledge-centric memory graph can serve as an effective, pluggable long-term memory for diverse LLM agents. By organizing memory around abstract decision-relevant knowledge instead of raw experiences or text chunks, PlugMem improves retrieval relevance, efficiency, and performance across varied tasks, demonstrating that a unified memory architecture can be both general and high-performing.

title: TTSR: Test-Time Self-Reflection for Continual Reasoning Improvement
summary: Test-time Training enables model adaptation using only test questions and offers a promising paradigm for improving the reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs). However, it faces two major challenges: test questions are often highly difficult, making self-generated pseudo-labels unreliable, and existing methods lack effective mechanisms to adapt to a model's specific reasoning weaknesses, leading to inefficient learning. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{TTSR}, a self-reflective test-time self-evolving training framework. TTSR employs a single pretrained language model that alternates between the roles of a \textit{Student} and a \textit{Teacher} at test time. The Student focuses on solving problems and learning from synthesized variant questions, while the Teacher analyzes the Student's failed reasoning trajectories, summarizes recurring reasoning weaknesses, and synthesizes targeted variant questions accordingly. This process guides the model to improve within a learnable regime through a continual self-evolving loop. Experimental results on multiple challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that TTSR consistently improves reasoning performance and generalizes well across different model backbones and general-domain reasoning tasks. These findings suggest that teacher-mediated self-reflection provides an effective pathway for stable and continual reasoning improvement at test time.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TTSR, a test-time self-training framework where a single LLM alternates as Student and Teacher to self-reflect on failed reasoning, generate targeted variant questions, and continually improve reasoning performance during testing.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time training approaches for LLM reasoning struggle because test questions are very hard (so self-generated labels are unreliable) and they do not adapt to the model’s specific reasoning weaknesses, leading to inefficient learning. The authors aim to design a more stable and targeted way for LLMs to improve their reasoning at test time without extra labeled data.
AI.method: TTSR uses a single pretrained LLM that alternates between a Student and a Teacher role at test time. The Student attempts to solve problems and learns from synthesized variant questions. The Teacher inspects the Student’s failed reasoning trajectories, identifies recurring reasoning weaknesses, and generates new variant questions tailored to those weaknesses. This creates a continual self-evolving loop where the model is trained on a curated, easier-to-learn subset of data that is adaptively focused on its deficiencies.
AI.result: On multiple challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, TTSR yields consistent improvements in reasoning performance and shows good generalization across different underlying LLM backbones and to general-domain reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-mediated self-reflection at test time, implemented via a single LLM alternating Student/Teacher roles, is an effective strategy for stable, continual improvement in reasoning performance using only test data and synthesized questions, without needing external supervision.

title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
summary: Web security demands rapid response capabilities to evolving cyber threats. Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises automation, but the need for trustworthy security responses is of the utmost importance. This work investigates the role of semantic relations in extracting information for sensitive operational tasks, such as configuring security controls for mitigating threats. To this end, it proposes to leverage hypernym-hyponym textual relations to extract relevant information from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports. By leveraging a neuro-symbolic approach, the multi-agent system automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system creating firewall rules to block malicious network traffic. Experimental results show the superior performance of the hypernym-hyponym retrieval strategy compared to various baselines and the higher effectiveness of the agentic approach in mitigating threats.

AI.tldr: Proposes a neuro-symbolic, multi-agent AI system that uses hypernym-hyponym semantic relations to automatically extract information from CTI reports and generate CLIPS-based firewall rules, achieving better retrieval and threat mitigation than baselines.
AI.motivation: Web security requires fast, automated, yet trustworthy responses to rapidly evolving cyber threats. Traditional manual analysis of Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports is too slow and error-prone for configuring operational security controls like firewalls. Existing AI-based automation often lacks fine-grained semantic understanding and reliability for sensitive tasks. The paper is motivated by the need to build agentic AI systems that can robustly interpret CTI text and translate it into correct, actionable security configurations.
AI.method: The authors design a neuro-symbolic, multi-agent system that processes CTI reports to configure firewall rules. The key idea is to exploit hypernym-hyponym semantic relations in the text to better identify and generalize relevant threat information. A retrieval component focuses on these relations to extract pertinent entities and concepts (e.g., types of attacks, malicious IPs, protocols). The system then automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system, which in turn produces firewall rules to block malicious traffic. The approach is evaluated against various baseline retrieval and extraction strategies.
AI.result: Experiments show that the hypernym-hyponym-based retrieval strategy outperforms multiple baselines in extracting relevant information from CTI reports. In downstream evaluation, the resulting multi-agent, neuro-symbolic pipeline achieves higher effectiveness in mitigating threats, as measured by the quality and impact of the automatically generated firewall rules compared to alternative approaches.
AI.conclusion: Semantic relations, specifically hypernym-hyponym structures, significantly improve information extraction from CTI reports for security automation. Coupling this semantic retrieval with a neuro-symbolic, multi-agent architecture enables reliable generation of CLIPS-based expert system rules for configuring firewalls. Overall, the agentic approach enhances both the accuracy of threat information extraction and the practical effectiveness of automated threat mitigation in web security contexts.

title: Generative AI in Managerial Decision-Making: Redefining Boundaries through Ambiguity Resolution and Sycophancy Analysis
summary: Generative artificial intelligence is increasingly being integrated into complex business workflows, fundamentally shifting the boundaries of managerial decision-making. However, the reliability of its strategic advice in ambiguous business contexts remains a critical knowledge gap. This study addresses this by comparing various models on ambiguity detection, evaluating how a systematic resolution process enhances response quality, and investigating their sycophantic behavior when presented with flawed directives. Using a novel four-dimensional business ambiguity taxonomy, we conducted a human-in-the-loop experiment across strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios. The resulting decisions were assessed with an "LLM-as-a-judge" framework on criteria including agreement, actionability, justification quality, and constraint adherence. Results reveal distinct performance capabilities. While models excel in detecting internal contradictions and contextual ambiguities, they struggle with structural linguistic nuances. Ambiguity resolution consistently increased response quality across all decision types, while sycophantic behavior analysis revealed distinct patterns depending on the model architecture. This study contributes to the bounded rationality literature by positioning GAI as a cognitive scaffold that can detect and resolve ambiguities managers might overlook, but whose own artificial limitations necessitate human management to ensure its reliability as a strategic partner.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well different generative AI models detect and resolve ambiguity in business decisions, and how often they behave sycophantically when given flawed instructions, showing that ambiguity resolution improves decision quality but models still need human oversight.
AI.motivation: As generative AI is embedded into complex business workflows, managers increasingly rely on it for strategic advice. Yet its reliability in ambiguous, high-stakes, or ill-defined business contexts is poorly understood. There is a need to know whether AI can recognize and clarify ambiguity instead of blindly following unclear or flawed managerial prompts, and how its limitations affect its role as a decision-support tool within the bounded rationality framework.
AI.method: The authors develop a four-dimensional taxonomy of business ambiguity and run a human-in-the-loop experiment covering strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios. Different generative AI models are tasked with identifying ambiguity, going through a structured ambiguity-resolution process, and responding to business problems. They are also tested for sycophantic behavior by being given flawed or misleading directives. An LLM-as-a-judge framework scores the outputs on agreement with ground truth or expert expectations, actionability, justification quality, and adherence to constraints.
AI.result: Models are generally good at detecting explicit internal contradictions and contextual inconsistencies but perform worse on subtler, structural linguistic ambiguities. Applying a systematic ambiguity-resolution process improves the quality of AI-generated decisions across all types of business tasks. The sycophancy analysis shows that models differ in how readily they go along with flawed instructions, with notable variation linked to model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI can act as a cognitive scaffold for managers by flagging and helping to resolve business ambiguities that humans might miss, thereby extending bounded rationality. However, its own limitations—particularly around nuanced linguistic ambiguity and a tendency toward sycophancy in some models—mean that human oversight and active management of the AI’s role are essential if it is to function as a reliable strategic partner.

title: How LLMs Cite and Why It Matters: A Cross-Model Audit of Reference Fabrication in AI-Assisted Academic Writing and Methods to Detect Phantom Citations
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have been noted to fabricate scholarly citations, yet the scope of this behavior across providers, domains, and prompting conditions remains poorly quantified. We present one of the largest citation hallucination audits to date, in which 10 commercially deployed LLMs were prompted across four academic domains, generating 69,557 citation instances verified against three scholarly databases (namely, CrossRef, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar). Our results show that the observed hallucination rates span a fivefold range (between 11.4% and 56.8%) and are strongly shaped by model, domain, and prompt framing. Our results also show that no model spontaneously generates citations when unprompted, which seems to establish hallucination as prompt-induced rather than intrinsic. We identify two practical filters: 1) multi-model consensus (with more than 3 LLMs citing the same work yields 95.6% accuracy, a 5.8-fold improvement), and 2) within-prompt repetition (with more than 2 replications yields 88.9% accuracy). In addition, we present findings on generational model tracking, which reveal that improvements are not guaranteed when deploying newer LLMs, and on capacity scaling, which appears to reduce hallucination within model families. Finally, a lightweight classifier trained solely on bibliographic string features is developed to classify hallucinated citations from verified citations, achieving AUC 0.876 in cross-validation and 0.834 in LOMO generalization (without querying any external database). This classifier offers a pre-screening tool deployable at inference time.

AI.tldr: The paper audits how often different commercial LLMs fabricate academic citations and proposes practical ways to detect and reduce these hallucinations.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for academic tasks and are known to sometimes invent plausible‑looking but fake references. However, there has been little systematic, large‑scale, cross‑model measurement of how prevalent citation hallucinations are, how they vary by domain and prompting, and how they might be mitigated or detected automatically. This gap makes it hard for users and developers to understand and manage the risks of relying on LLM‑generated citations.
AI.method: The authors conduct a large audit of 10 commercially deployed LLMs across four academic domains. They design prompts that elicit citations under different framings, collect 69,557 citation instances, and automatically verify each citation against three major scholarly databases: CrossRef, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar. They then quantify hallucination rates by model, domain, and prompting condition. They also analyze whether models generate citations without being explicitly asked, study how agreement across multiple models and repetition within a single prompt relate to citation validity, examine generational and scaling trends within model families, and train a lightweight classifier using only bibliographic string features to distinguish hallucinated from verified citations, evaluating it with cross‑validation and leave‑one‑model‑out (LOMO) tests.
AI.result: Hallucination rates differ substantially across models, ranging from 11.4% to 56.8%, and are strongly influenced by the specific LLM, academic domain, and prompt framing. None of the models produce citations when not explicitly prompted, indicating that hallucinated citations are induced by citation‑seeking prompts.
