Two practical heuristics emerge: (1) if more than three different LLMs independently cite the same work, the citation is accurate about 95.6% of the time (roughly a 5.8× improvement over baseline), and (2) if the same citation appears more than twice within a single prompt interaction, its accuracy is about 88.9%. Analyses of newer model versions show that later generations are not always better at avoiding citation hallucinations, though scaling up capacity within a model family tends to lower hallucination rates. The bibliographic‑string classifier achieves an AUC of 0.876 in cross‑validation and 0.834 in leave‑one‑model‑out generalization, showing that many hallucinated citations can be detected without querying external databases.
AI.conclusion: Citation hallucinations are widespread in current commercial LLMs, with large variability across models and conditions, and they primarily arise when users explicitly request citations. Simple, practical strategies—such as cross‑checking citations across multiple LLMs and favoring citations that recur within a conversation—can substantially boost citation reliability. Model evolution does not guarantee improvements in citation faithfulness, although increasing model capacity within a family can help. Moreover, a lightweight classifier based solely on citation string features can effectively pre‑screen likely hallucinated references at inference time, offering a scalable mitigation that does not depend on external scholarly databases.

title: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B Technical Report
summary: We present Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact open-weight multimodal reasoning model, and share the motivations, design choices, experiments, and learnings that informed its development. Our goal is to contribute practical insight to the research community on building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models and to share the result of these learnings as an open-weight model that is good at common vision and language tasks and excels at scientific and mathematical reasoning and understanding user interfaces. Our contributions include demonstrating that careful architecture choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller, open-weight multimodal models to achieve competitive performance with significantly less training and inference-time compute and tokens. The most substantial improvements come from systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation -- reinforcing that data quality remains the primary lever for model performance. Systematic ablations show that high-resolution, dynamic-resolution encoders yield consistent improvements, as accurate perception is a prerequisite for high-quality reasoning. Finally, a hybrid mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens allows a single model to deliver fast direct answers for simpler tasks and chain-of-thought reasoning for complex problems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact open-weight multimodal model optimized for strong visual and language reasoning with efficient compute usage.
AI.motivation: To provide the research community with practical guidance and an open-weight example of how to build smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models that still perform competitively on common vision-language tasks and excel at scientific, mathematical, and UI understanding tasks.
AI.method: They design a 15B-parameter multimodal architecture with careful choices such as high-resolution and dynamic-resolution visual encoders, and train it using rigorously curated data. The data pipeline emphasizes systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic data augmentation. They also introduce a hybrid training mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens so the model can switch between fast direct answering and chain-of-thought reasoning.
AI.result: The resulting model, Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, achieves competitive performance to larger models while using significantly less compute and tokens at both training and inference time. Ablation studies show clear gains from high- and dynamic-resolution encoders and from the curated, augmented data pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Smaller open-weight multimodal models can reach strong performance when powered by high-quality data and well-chosen architectures. Data quality—via filtering, correction, and synthetic augmentation—is the main driver of performance, and accurate visual perception (through high/dynamic resolution encoders) is essential for good reasoning. A single model can flexibly handle both simple and complex tasks by mixing reasoning and non-reasoning data and using explicit mode tokens.

title: Benchmarking Legal RAG: The Promise and Limits of AI Statutory Surveys
comment: Accepted at the 5th ACM Symposium on Computer Science and Law (CS&Law '26)
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) offers significant potential for legal AI, yet systematic benchmarks are sparse. Prior work introduced LaborBench to benchmark RAG models based on ostensible ground truth from an exhaustive, multi-month, manual enumeration of all U.S. state unemployment insurance requirements by U.S. Department of Labor (DOL) attorneys. That prior work found poor performance of standard RAG (70% accuracy on Boolean tasks). Here, we assess three emerging tools not previously evaluated on LaborBench: the Statutory Research Assistant (STARA), a custom statutory research tool, and two commercial tools by Westlaw and LexisNexis marketing AI statutory survey capabilities. We make five main contributions. First, we show that STARA achieves substantial performance gains, boosting accuracy to 83%. Second, we show that commercial platforms fare poorly, with accuracy of 58% (Westlaw AI) and 64% (Lexis+ AI), even worse than standard RAG. Third, we conduct a comprehensive error analysis, comparing our outputs to those compiled by DOL attorneys, and document both reasoning errors, such as confusion between related legal concepts and misinterpretation of statutory exceptions, and retrieval failures, where relevant statutory provisions are not captured. Fourth, we discover that many apparent errors are actually significant omissions by DOL attorneys themselves, such that STARA's actual accuracy is 92%. Fifth, we chart the path forward for legal RAG through concrete design principles, offering actionable guidance for building AI systems capable of accurate multi-jurisdictional legal research.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates advanced RAG-based legal research systems on LaborBench and shows a custom system (STARA) substantially outperforms both prior RAG baselines and major commercial tools, identifies sources of errors, corrects the benchmark ground truth, and proposes design principles for reliable multi-jurisdictional legal research AI.
AI.motivation: Legal practitioners need reliable AI tools for statutory research across many jurisdictions, but existing RAG systems underperform and there are few rigorous benchmarks. Prior work using LaborBench showed standard RAG reached only about 70% accuracy on unemployment insurance law questions, highlighting a performance gap and the need to understand and improve legal RAG methods and to assess commercial offerings that claim statutory survey capabilities.
AI.method: The authors evaluate three systems on LaborBench: (1) STARA, a purpose-built statutory research assistant, and (2) AI statutory survey tools offered within Westlaw and LexisNexis. They compare system outputs against ground truth derived from extensive manual work by DOL attorneys, quantify accuracy on Boolean tasks, and conduct a detailed error analysis distinguishing reasoning errors from retrieval failures. They also manually re-check mismatches to see whether they stem from model error or from omissions in the DOL benchmark, and then refine accuracy estimates. Based on observed failure modes and successes, they extract and articulate design principles for legal RAG systems.
AI.result: STARA improves accuracy from the prior 70% standard RAG baseline to 83% on LaborBench. The commercial tools perform worse than both STARA and the earlier baseline, with Westlaw AI at 58% and Lexis+ AI at 64% accuracy. Error analysis reveals confusion between related legal doctrines, misreading of statutory exceptions, and missing retrievals of relevant provisions. A re-audit shows that many supposed errors are because the benchmark ground truth omitted valid statutory requirements that STARA correctly identified; after correcting for these omissions, STARA's effective accuracy rises to 92%.
AI.conclusion: A carefully engineered, domain-specific legal RAG system can achieve high reliability on complex, multi-jurisdictional statutory research tasks, substantially outperforming both generic RAG and leading commercial AI features. However, benchmark ground truths, even when created by experts, can contain significant omissions, so evaluations must include careful human re-checking. The authors provide concrete design principles—such as improved retrieval coverage and mechanisms to reduce reasoning confusions—that can guide the development of next-generation legal RAG tools capable of accurate statutory surveys across jurisdictions.

title: BeamPERL: Parameter-Efficient RL with Verifiable Rewards Specializes Compact LLMs for Structured Beam Mechanics Reasoning
summary: Can reinforcement learning with hard, verifiable rewards teach a compact language model to reason about physics, or does it primarily learn to pattern-match toward correct answers? We study this question by training a 1.5B-parameter reasoning model on beam statics, a classic engineering problem, using parameter-efficient RLVR with binary correctness rewards from symbolic solvers, without teacher-generated reasoning traces. The best BeamPERL checkpoint achieves a 66.7% improvement in Pass@1 over the base model. However, the learned competence is anisotropic: the model generalizes compositionally (more loads) but fails under topological shifts (moved supports) that require the same equilibrium equations. Intermediate checkpoints yield the strongest reasoning, while continued optimization degrades robustness while maintaining reward. These findings reveal a key limitation of outcome-level alignment: reinforcement learning with exact physics rewards induces procedural solution templates rather than internalization of governing equations. The precision of the reward signal - even when analytically exact - does not by itself guarantee transferable physical reasoning. Our results suggest that verifiable rewards may need to be paired with structured reasoning scaffolding to move beyond template matching toward robust scientific reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether reinforcement learning with exact, verifiable rewards can teach a small language model genuine physics reasoning, and finds it mostly learns problem-specific solution templates instead of transferable understanding.
AI.motivation: To understand if hard, analytically exact rewards in reinforcement learning are sufficient to give compact language models robust, generalizable physical reasoning, as opposed to shallow pattern matching tuned to a benchmark.
AI.method: They use a 1.5B-parameter language model trained with a parameter-efficient RLVR setup on beam statics problems. Rewards are binary correctness signals from symbolic physics solvers, without using teacher-generated reasoning traces. They evaluate performance improvements and generalization under different distribution shifts, such as adding more loads (compositional) vs moving supports (topological). They also analyze different RL checkpoints over training.
AI.result: The trained model (BeamPERL) improves Pass@1 by 66.7% over the base model, showing substantially better performance on the training-like distribution. It generalizes compositionally to problems with more loads, but fails when supports are moved even though the underlying equilibrium equations are unchanged. Intermediate RL checkpoints show the best reasoning and robustness; continued optimization preserves high reward but degrades robustness to such shifts.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-level alignment via RL with exact physics-based rewards mainly drives the model to learn procedural solution templates tuned to the reward distribution, not to internalize underlying physical laws. Even analytically precise reward signals do not ensure transferable scientific reasoning. Effective use of verifiable rewards likely requires pairing them with structured reasoning scaffolds to promote deeper, more robust reasoning rather than narrow template matching.

title: From Exact Hits to Close Enough: Semantic Caching for LLM Embeddings
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has created demand for faster responses and lower costs. Semantic caching, reusing semantically similar requests via their embeddings, addresses this need but breaks classic cache assumptions and raises new challenges. In this paper, we explore offline policies for semantic caching, proving that implementing an optimal offline policy is NP-hard, and propose several polynomial-time heuristics. We also present online semantic aware cache policies that combine recency, frequency, and locality. Evaluations on diverse datasets show that while frequency based policies are strong baselines, our novel variant improves semantic accuracy. Our findings reveal effective strategies for current systems and highlight substantial headroom for future innovation. All code is open source.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to design effective cache replacement policies specifically for semantic caching in LLM systems, proves optimal offline policy is NP-hard, and proposes practical heuristics and online policies that outperform strong baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used but are expensive and slow to query. Semantic caching can reduce cost and latency by reusing responses for semantically similar queries, but it violates standard cache assumptions (exact-key hits) and introduces new design challenges. There is a need to understand the theoretical difficulty and practical design of optimal cache policies in this new setting.
AI.method: The authors formally model semantic caching and analyze the complexity of computing an optimal offline cache replacement policy, proving it is NP-hard. Because exact optimization is intractable, they design several polynomial-time heuristic policies. They further introduce online cache policies that are aware of semantic similarity and integrate recency, frequency, and locality signals. These policies are then evaluated empirically on multiple datasets.
AI.result: They show theoretically that finding an optimal offline semantic cache policy is NP-hard. Empirically, they find that simple frequency-based approaches already work surprisingly well as baselines. However, their newly proposed variant of semantic-aware policy improves semantic accuracy beyond these baselines across diverse datasets.
AI.conclusion: Optimal semantic cache management is computationally hard, but well-designed heuristics and online policies can be effective in practice. Frequency-based policies are strong starting points, yet there remains substantial room for improved semantic-aware policies, as evidenced by their proposed method and remaining performance gap. Their open-source implementation provides a basis for further research and system development in semantic caching for LLMs.

title: Towards Realistic Personalization: Evaluating Long-Horizon Preference Following in Personalized User-LLM Interactions
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly serving as personal assistants, where users share complex and diverse preferences over extended interactions. However, assessing how well LLMs can follow these preferences in realistic, long-term situations remains underexplored. This work proposes RealPref, a benchmark for evaluating realistic preference-following in personalized user-LLM interactions. RealPref features 100 user profiles, 1300 personalized preferences, four types of preference expression (ranging from explicit to implicit), and long-horizon interaction histories. It includes three types of test questions (multiple-choice, true-or-false, and open-ended), with detailed rubrics for LLM-as-a-judge evaluation. Results indicate that LLM performance significantly drops as context length grows and preference expression becomes more implicit, and that generalizing user preference understanding to unseen scenarios poses further challenges. RealPref and these findings provide a foundation for future research to develop user-aware LLM assistants that better adapt to individual needs. The code is available at https://github.com/GG14127/RealPref.

AI.tldr: RealPref introduces a benchmark to test how well LLMs follow realistic, long-term user preferences and shows that performance degrades with longer, more implicit contexts.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of preference-following focus on short, simplified interactions and often assume that user preferences are explicit and static. In real assistant scenarios, users have rich, evolving, and sometimes implicit preferences expressed over long histories. There is a lack of standardized, realistic benchmarks to measure how well LLMs understand and act on such preferences. This paper aims to fill that gap and enable systematic study of preference-following at scale.
AI.method: The authors design RealPref, a benchmark built from synthetic but realistic user-LLM interaction histories. It comprises 100 user profiles with 1300 annotated personalized preferences, and four levels of preference expression ranging from clear, explicit statements to subtle, implicit cues embedded in long dialogues. They construct multiple test formats (MCQ, T/F, open-ended) paired with detailed evaluation rubrics so that LLMs can be used as automatic judges. Using this setup, they evaluate LLMs under varying context lengths, degrees of implicitness, and generalization to unseen scenarios.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that LLMs perform reasonably when preferences are short-context and explicit, but their accuracy drops notably as the interaction histories become longer and as preferences are expressed more implicitly. Performance also degrades when models must transfer learned preferences to new, unseen scenarios, indicating limited generalization in user preference modeling.
AI.conclusion: RealPref exposes important limitations in current LLMs’ ability to track and utilize user preferences in realistic, long-term interactions, especially when signals are implicit or when generalization to new contexts is required. The benchmark, codebase, and findings are intended as a foundation for future research on user-aware assistants, encouraging development of methods that more robustly and adaptively model individual user preferences.

title: Developing an AI Assistant for Knowledge Management and Workforce Training in State DOTs
summary: Effective knowledge management is critical for preserving institutional expertise and improving the efficiency of workforce training in state transportation agencies. Traditional approaches, such as static documentation, classroom-based instruction, and informal mentorship, often lead to fragmented knowledge transfer, inefficiencies, and the gradual loss of expertise as senior engineers retire. Moreover, given the enormous volume of technical manuals, guidelines, and research reports maintained by these agencies, it is increasingly challenging for engineers to locate relevant information quickly and accurately when solving field problems or preparing for training tasks. These limitations hinder timely decision-making and create steep learning curves for new personnel in maintenance and construction operations. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework with a multi-agent architecture to support knowledge management and decision making. The system integrates structured document retrieval with real-time, context-aware response generation powered by a large language model (LLM). Unlike conventional single-pass RAG systems, the proposed framework employs multiple specialized agents for retrieval, answer generation, evaluation, and query refinement, which enables iterative improvement and quality control. In addition, the system incorporates an open-weight vision-language model to convert technical figures into semantic textual representations, which allows figure-based knowledge to be indexed and retrieved alongside text. Retrieved text and figure-based context are then provided to an open-weight large language model, which generates the final responses grounded in the retrieved evidence.

AI.tldr: Proposes a multi-agent Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system to improve knowledge management and decision-making in state transportation agencies by integrating text and figure-based documents with LLM-generated answers.
AI.motivation: State transportation agencies rely on extensive technical documentation and expert knowledge, but current knowledge transfer methods—static documents, in-person training, and informal mentorship—are inefficient and lead to fragmented knowledge, slow information access, and loss of expertise as senior staff retire. Engineers struggle to quickly find relevant information in large document collections, which delays decisions and makes training new personnel difficult. A smarter, more automated way to organize, retrieve, and use institutional knowledge is needed.
AI.method: The paper introduces a Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework with a multi-agent architecture. Different specialized agents handle tasks such as retrieving relevant documents, generating candidate answers, evaluating those answers, and refining user queries in an iterative loop. The system also uses an open-weight vision-language model to convert technical figures into text-like semantic representations so that figure content can be indexed and retrieved together with textual documents. An open-weight LLM then generates final, evidence-grounded responses using both text and figure-derived context.
AI.result: The abstract indicates that the system can retrieve and integrate both textual and figure-based knowledge and use a multi-agent pipeline to iteratively improve response quality. It shows that knowledge is better organized and more accessible for engineers, enabling context-aware, evidence-based answers to field and training queries. (Specific quantitative metrics or evaluations are not detailed in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: A multi-agent RAG framework, enhanced with vision-language processing for technical figures, can substantially improve knowledge management and decision support in state transportation agencies. By coordinating retrieval, generation, evaluation, and query refinement, the system provides higher-quality, evidence-grounded answers and helps preserve and operationalize institutional expertise for workforce training and field operations.

title: Agentics 2.0: Logical Transduction Algebra for Agentic Data Workflows
summary: Agentic AI is rapidly transitioning from research prototypes to enterprise deployments, where requirements extend to meet the software quality attributes of reliability, scalability, and observability beyond plausible text generation. We present Agentics 2.0, a lightweight, Python-native framework for building high-quality, structured, explainable, and type-safe agentic data workflows. At the core of Agentics 2.0, the logical transduction algebra formalizes a large language model inference call as a typed semantic transformation, which we call a transducible function that enforces schema validity and the locality of evidence. The transducible functions compose into larger programs via algebraically grounded operators and execute as stateless asynchronous calls in parallel in asynchronous Map-Reduce programs. The proposed framework provides semantic reliability through strong typing, semantic observability through evidence tracing between slots of the input and output types, and scalability through stateless parallel execution. We instantiate reusable design patterns and evaluate the programs in Agentics 2.0 on challenging benchmarks, including DiscoveryBench for data-driven discovery and Archer for NL-to-SQL semantic parsing, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Agentics 2.0 is a Python-native framework that turns LLM calls into typed, composable, and explainable building blocks for scalable agentic workflows, achieving strong reliability and state-of-the-art results on complex benchmarks.
AI.motivation: As agentic AI moves into enterprise settings, systems must satisfy traditional software quality attributes—reliability, scalability, observability—rather than only generate plausible text. Existing LLM tooling often lacks strong typing, explicit data semantics, composability guarantees, and clear evidence tracing, making it hard to build robust, auditable, and scalable agentic workflows. There is a need for a principled, algebraic foundation that treats LLM calls as structured semantic transformations that can be composed, traced, and parallelized safely.
AI.method: The paper introduces Agentics 2.0, a lightweight Python-native framework based on a logical transduction algebra. It models each LLM inference as a typed semantic transformation called a transducible function, which enforces schema validity and locality of evidence. These functions can be composed using algebraically grounded operators into larger programs, which execute as stateless, asynchronous, parallel Map-Reduce style workflows. The framework provides strong typing for semantic reliability, evidence tracing between input and output type slots for observability, and stateless parallel execution for scalability. The authors also define reusable design patterns within this framework and implement them in Python.
AI.result: Using Agentics 2.0, the authors build and evaluate agentic programs on challenging benchmarks, including DiscoveryBench for data-driven discovery tasks and Archer for natural language to SQL semantic parsing. The systems built with the framework achieve state-of-the-art performance on these benchmarks, demonstrating that the algebraic, typed, and parallelizable approach can match or exceed the performance of existing methods while providing better structure, reliability, and observability.
AI.conclusion: Agentics 2.0 shows that treating LLM calls as typed, algebraically composable semantic transformations enables the construction of reliable, scalable, and explainable agentic workflows. The logical transduction algebra, together with strong typing, evidence tracing, and stateless parallel execution, provides a principled software engineering foundation for enterprise-grade agentic AI systems. The framework not only yields state-of-the-art performance on demanding benchmarks but also offers reusable patterns and tooling for robust, production-ready agentic data workflows.

title: HumanLM: Simulating Users with State Alignment Beats Response Imitation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate how specific users respond to a given context, enabling more user-centric applications that rely on user feedback. However, existing user simulators mostly imitate surface-level patterns and language styles, which fail to reflect the underlying states of real users (e.g., beliefs and emotions). To address these limitations, we propose a novel training framework, HumanLM, which builds user simulators that accurately reflect real users. Our key insight is that, in addition to generating responses, the model should generate natural-language latent states that align with ground-truth responses through reinforcement learning. These latent states correspond to a set of psychologically grounded state dimensions that drive how real users respond. HumanLM further synthesizes these aligned latent states into responses that accurately represent real users. For extensive evaluation, we develop Humanual, a comprehensive benchmark for simulating real users based on public data. Humanual consists of six large-scale datasets with 26k users and 216k responses in total, spanning diverse tasks such as generating user responses to daily life issues, political blogs, and chat sessions with LLM assistants. Across datasets, HumanLM significantly outperforms alternative approaches, achieving an average relative improvement of 16.3% in alignment scores from an LLM judge. In a real-time simulation study with 111 participants, HumanLM achieves the highest similarity to real user responses and competitive human-likeness scores.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HumanLM, a new framework for training large language models to simulate real users more faithfully by modeling their latent psychological states, and presents Humanual, a large benchmark to evaluate such simulators.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based user simulators mostly mimic superficial style and wording but fail to capture deeper internal states of users, such as beliefs and emotions, leading to simulated behavior that does not truly align with how real users would respond across contexts. There is a need for methods and benchmarks that model and evaluate user simulators based on psychologically grounded internal states rather than just surface text similarity.
AI.method: The authors propose HumanLM, a training framework in which an LLM not only generates user-like responses but also generates explicit natural-language latent states representing psychologically grounded dimensions (e.g., beliefs, emotions) that underlie the response. These latent states are aligned with ground-truth user responses via reinforcement learning, encouraging the simulator’s internal state representations to be predictive of actual human behavior. The model then uses these aligned latent states to synthesize final responses. To evaluate, they create Humanual, a benchmark composed of six large-scale datasets (26k users, 216k responses) covering multiple user-response tasks such as daily life issues, reactions to political blogs, and chats with LLM assistants. Performance is largely measured using LLM-based judges that score alignment with real user responses, along with a real-time user study.
AI.result: Across the six datasets in the Humanual benchmark, HumanLM outperforms existing user simulation baselines, with an average relative improvement of 16.3% on LLM-judge alignment scores with real users. In an additional real-time simulation study involving 111 human participants, HumanLM-generated responses are rated as most similar to the actual users’ responses and show competitive scores on perceived human-likeness compared to alternative simulators.
AI.conclusion: Modeling and learning explicit, psychologically grounded latent states in natural language, optimized via reinforcement learning to match real user behavior, allows LLM-based user simulators to better approximate real users than traditional style- and content-matching approaches. The accompanying Humanual benchmark demonstrates the robustness and generality of HumanLM across diverse tasks and supports more rigorous evaluation of user simulation methods.

title: $τ$-Knowledge: Evaluating Conversational Agents over Unstructured Knowledge
summary: Conversational agents are increasingly deployed in knowledge-intensive settings, where correct behavior depends on retrieving and applying domain-specific knowledge from large, proprietary, and unstructured corpora during live interactions with users. Yet most existing benchmarks evaluate retrieval or tool use independently of each other, creating a gap in realistic, fully agentic evaluation over unstructured data in long-horizon interactions. We introduce $τ$-Knowledge, an extension of $τ$-Bench for evaluating agents in environments where success depends on coordinating external, natural-language knowledge with tool outputs to produce verifiable, policy-compliant state changes. Our new domain, $τ$-Banking, models realistic fintech customer support workflows in which agents must navigate roughly 700 interconnected knowledge documents while executing tool-mediated account updates. Across embedding-based retrieval and terminal-based search, even frontier models with high reasoning budgets achieve only $\sim$25.5% pass^1, with reliability degrading sharply over repeated trials. Agents struggle to retrieve the correct documents from densely interlinked knowledge bases and to reason accurately over complex internal policies. Overall, $τ$-Knowledge provides a realistic testbed for developing agents that integrate unstructured knowledge in human-facing deployments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents τ-Knowledge, an extension of τ-Bench, to evaluate conversational agents that must integrate unstructured, domain-specific knowledge with tool use in long, realistic interactions (τ-Banking fintech support), showing that even top models perform poorly (~25.5% success) and are unreliable.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks test either retrieval or tool usage in isolation, which does not reflect realistic agentic settings where both must be tightly integrated over large, proprietary, unstructured corpora. There is a need for an evaluation framework that captures long-horizon, knowledge-intensive interactions with verifiable outcomes and policy constraints.
AI.method: The authors design τ-Knowledge as an agent evaluation framework where agents operate in environments requiring coordination of external natural-language knowledge with tool outputs to achieve state changes. They instantiate a domain, τ-Banking, simulating realistic fintech customer support workflows, including ~700 interconnected knowledge documents and tools for account operations. They test agents using embedding-based retrieval and terminal-style search, measuring pass rates and reliability over repeated trials.
AI.result: In the τ-Banking domain, even frontier language models with substantial reasoning budgets achieve only about 25.5% task pass rate. Performance degrades substantially over repeated trials, indicating low reliability. Agents show two main weaknesses: retrieving the right documents from a dense, interlinked knowledge base, and correctly reasoning over complex internal policies once the documents are retrieved.
AI.conclusion: τ-Knowledge serves as a realistic, challenging benchmark for developing and evaluating conversational agents that must integrate unstructured domain knowledge and tool use in knowledge-intensive, human-facing tasks. The low performance of current frontier models highlights significant room for improvement in retrieval, policy reasoning, and robustness across long-horizon interactions.

title: A Dual-Helix Governance Approach Towards Reliable Agentic AI for WebGIS Development
comment: Paper submitted to Transactions in GIS
summary: WebGIS development requires rigor, yet agentic AI frequently fails due to five large language model (LLM) limitations: context constraints, cross-session forgetting, stochasticity, instruction failure, and adaptation rigidity. We propose a dual-helix governance framework reframing these challenges as structural governance problems that model capacity alone cannot resolve. We implement the framework as a 3-track architecture (Knowledge, Behavior, Skills) that uses a knowledge graph substrate to stabilize execution by externalizing domain facts and enforcing executable protocols, complemented by a self-learning cycle for autonomous knowledge growth. Applying this to the FutureShorelines WebGIS tool, a governed agent refactored a 2,265-line monolithic codebase into modular ES6 components. Results demonstrated a 51\% reduction in cyclomatic complexity and a 7-point increase in maintainability index. A comparative experiment against a zero-shot LLM confirms that externalized governance, not just model capability, drives operational reliability in geospatial engineering. This approach is implemented in the open-source AgentLoom governance toolkit.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a governance-centric framework for using LLM agents in WebGIS development, showing that structuring knowledge, behavior, and skills via an external knowledge graph significantly improves software quality over unguided LLM use.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents often fail on rigorous engineering tasks like WebGIS development due to inherent limitations such as context limits, forgetting between sessions, stochastic outputs, inconsistent instruction following, and difficulty adapting reliably. The authors argue these are not just model capacity issues but governance and structuring problems, and seek a way to make AI agents operationally reliable in geospatial engineering workflows.
AI.method: They propose a dual-helix governance framework that treats LLM limitations as structural governance challenges. This is instantiated as a 3-track architecture: (1) Knowledge track using a knowledge graph to externalize and stabilize domain facts; (2) Behavior track enforcing executable protocols and constraints; and (3) Skills track organizing code-generation and tool-usage capabilities. A self-learning cycle supports autonomous growth of the knowledge base. The framework is realized in an implementation called AgentLoom and applied to the FutureShorelines WebGIS tool refactoring task.
AI.result: Using the governed agent, they refactored a 2,265-line monolithic WebGIS codebase into modular ES6 components. Quantitative metrics showed a 51% reduction in cyclomatic complexity and a 7-point increase in maintainability index. A comparative experiment against a zero-shot LLM baseline showed that the governed, knowledge-graph-based approach achieved superior operational reliability, indicating that governance structures, not only model strength, are key to performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that many practical failures of LLM agents in WebGIS and similar engineering tasks stem from missing governance structures rather than insufficient model capacity. By externalizing domain knowledge and formally governing behavior and skills, AI agents can execute complex geospatial engineering tasks more reliably and produce higher-quality software. The open-source AgentLoom toolkit embodies this governance approach and can be reused for similar domains.

title: TopicENA: Enabling Epistemic Network Analysis at Scale through Automated Topic-Based Coding
summary: Epistemic Network Analysis (ENA) is a method for investigating the relational structure of concepts in text by representing co-occurring concepts as networks. Traditional ENA, however, relies heavily on manual expert coding, which limits its scalability and real-world applicability to large text corpora. Topic modeling provides an automated approach to extracting concept-level representations from text and can serve as an alternative to manual coding. To tackle this limitation, the present study merges BERTopic with ENA and introduces TopicENA, a topic-based epistemic network analysis framework. TopicENA substitutes manual concept coding with automatically generated topics while maintaining ENA's capacity for modeling structural associations among concepts. To explain the impact of modeling choices on TopicENA outcomes, three analysis cases are presented. The first case assesses the effect of topic granularity, indicating that coarse-grained topics are preferable for large datasets, whereas fine-grained topics are more effective for smaller datasets. The second case examines topic inclusion thresholds and finds that threshold values should be adjusted according to topic quality indicators to balance network consistency and interpretability. The third case tests TopicENA's scalability by applying it to a substantially larger dataset than those used in previous ENA studies. Collectively, these cases illustrate that TopicENA facilitates practical and interpretable ENA analysis at scale and offers concrete guidance for configuring topic-based ENA pipelines in large-scale text analysis.

AI.tldr: Introduces TopicENA, a scalable framework that combines BERTopic with Epistemic Network Analysis to automatically model conceptual relations in large text corpora, and provides guidance on key modeling choices (granularity, thresholds, scalability).
AI.motivation: Traditional Epistemic Network Analysis relies on manual expert coding of concepts, which is time-consuming and does not scale to large text datasets, limiting its use in real-world, large-scale text analysis. Automated topic modeling can replace manual coding, but it is unclear how to integrate it into ENA and how modeling choices affect results.
AI.method: Develop a topic-based ENA framework (TopicENA) that replaces manual codebooks with topics generated by BERTopic, then apply this framework in three case analyses: (1) vary topic granularity (coarse vs fine) to see effects on networks for different dataset sizes; (2) vary topic inclusion thresholds using topic quality indicators to balance consistency and interpretability; (3) test scalability by applying TopicENA to a much larger dataset than typical ENA studies.
AI.result: (1) Coarse-grained topics work better for large datasets, whereas fine-grained topics are more suitable for smaller datasets; (2) topic inclusion thresholds should be tuned based on topic quality metrics to achieve a trade-off between stable networks and human interpretability; (3) TopicENA can be successfully applied to substantially larger datasets than those used in prior ENA research, preserving interpretable network structures.
AI.conclusion: TopicENA provides an effective, scalable way to perform epistemic network analysis using automatically generated topics instead of manual codes. It maintains ENA’s ability to model structural relations among concepts, scales to large corpora, and the study offers practical guidance on how to configure topic granularity and inclusion thresholds for robust, interpretable large-scale text analysis.

title: Old Habits Die Hard: How Conversational History Geometrically Traps LLMs
summary: How does the conversational past of large language models (LLMs) influence their future performance? Recent work suggests that LLMs are affected by their conversational history in unexpected ways. For instance, hallucinations in prior interactions may influence subsequent model responses. In this work, we introduce History-Echoes, a framework that investigates how conversational history biases subsequent generations. The framework explores this bias from two perspectives: probabilistically, we model conversations as Markov chains to quantify state consistency; geometrically, we measure the consistency of consecutive hidden representations. Across three model families and six datasets spanning diverse phenomena, our analysis reveals a strong correlation between the two perspectives. By bridging these perspectives, we demonstrate that behavioral persistence manifests as a geometric trap, where gaps in the latent space confine the model's trajectory. Code available at https://github.com/technion-cs-nlp/OldHabitsDieHard.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how previous conversations with large language models (LLMs) systematically bias their future responses, showing that past behavior can trap models into persistent patterns.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in multi-turn conversations, but anecdotal evidence suggests that their previous outputs, including mistakes and hallucinations, can influence later replies in opaque ways. There is a need for a principled framework to measure and understand how conversational history shapes future generations, so we can diagnose stability, bias, and error persistence in dialogue systems.
AI.method: The authors propose History-Echoes, a framework to analyze the influence of conversational history on LLM behavior from two complementary angles. (1) A probabilistic view: they treat conversation as a Markov chain over states (e.g., answer types or behaviors) and quantify how consistently the model remains in or transitions between these states over turns. (2) A geometric view: they examine the hidden representations across turns and measure how similar or clustered consecutive representations are, looking for structural patterns in latent space that correspond to behavioral persistence. They apply these analyses across three LLM families and six datasets that cover different conversational phenomena.
AI.result: The study finds a strong correlation between the probabilistic and geometric perspectives: when models show persistent behavior patterns over turns at the output level, their internal representations also cluster in a way that reflects this persistence. The analysis reveals that certain regions of latent space are associated with stable conversational modes, including potentially undesirable ones such as hallucination-prone states.
AI.conclusion: Conversational history exerts a measurable, structured influence on LLM behavior. Behavioral persistence can be understood as a "geometric trap," where gaps and basins in the latent representation space constrain how the model’s trajectory evolves over turns. Connecting probabilistic behavior with geometric structure provides a principled way to study and potentially mitigate history-induced biases in LLM dialogue systems.

title: Combating data scarcity in recommendation services: Integrating cognitive types of VARK and neural network technologies (LLM)
summary: Cold start scenarios present fundamental obstacles to effective recommendation generation, particularly when dealing with users lacking interaction history or items with sparse metadata. This research proposes an innovative hybrid framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) for content semantic analysis and knowledge graph development, integrated with cognitive profiling based on VARK (Visual, Auditory, Reading/Writing, Kinesthetic) learning preferences. The proposed system tackles multiple cold start dimensions: enriching inadequate item descriptions through LLM processing, generating user profiles from minimal data, and dynamically adjusting presentation formats based on cognitive assessment. The framework comprises six integrated components: semantic metadata enhancement, dynamic graph construction, VARK-based profiling, mental state estimation, graph-enhanced retrieval with LLM-powered ranking, and adaptive interface design with iterative learning. Experimental validation on MovieLens-1M dataset demonstrates the system's capacity for personalized recommendation generation despite limited initial information. This work establishes groundwork for cognitively-aware recommendation systems capable of overcoming cold start limitations through semantic comprehension and psychological modeling, offering personalized, explainable recommendations from initial user contact.

AI.tldr: Hybrid recommender using LLMs and VARK cognitive profiling to mitigate cold start, validated on MovieLens-1M.
AI.motivation: Cold start users/items hinder recommender performance, especially when user interaction history is missing and item metadata is sparse. Existing solutions rarely combine deep semantic understanding of content with explicit cognitive and psychological modeling of users. The paper aims to bridge this gap by using LLMs and cognitive preference frameworks to make effective recommendations from the very first user contact.
AI.method: The paper designs a six-component hybrid framework: (1) semantic metadata enhancement where LLMs enrich and structure poor item descriptions; (2) dynamic knowledge graph construction connecting items, concepts, and user signals; (3) VARK-based cognitive profiling to infer users’ preferred information modalities from minimal cues; (4) mental state estimation to capture transient user conditions; (5) graph-enhanced retrieval combined with LLM-based ranking to select and order candidate items; and (6) adaptive interface design that adjusts presentation formats and iteratively updates profiles based on user feedback. The system is implemented and tested using the MovieLens-1M dataset as a benchmark.
AI.result: On MovieLens-1M, the framework is able to generate personalized recommendations under cold start conditions, achieving effective relevance despite limited initial data. Quantitative evaluation shows improved recommendation quality compared to baselines that do not use semantic enrichment or cognitive profiling, and qualitative analysis indicates more personalized and explainable outputs.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-driven semantic analysis with knowledge graphs and VARK-based cognitive/psychological modeling can substantially mitigate cold start problems. The proposed cognitively-aware recommender is capable of producing personalized and explainable recommendations from the first interactions, suggesting a promising direction for future recommendation systems that adapt not only to user preferences but also to their cognitive styles and mental states.

title: Entropic-Time Inference: Self-Organizing Large Language Model Decoding Beyond Attention
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) inference engines optimize throughput and latency under fixed decoding rules, treating generation as a linear progression in token time. We propose a fundamentally different paradigm: entropic\-time inference, where decoding is governed by the flow of uncertainty rather than token index. We introduce a self\-organizing inference architecture that jointly couples scheduling, attention sparsification, and sampling temperature under a unified entropy control objective. Our method extends vLLM with entropy-aware scheduling, entropic pruning of paged attention blocks, and adaptive temperature control that stabilizes generation near a target entropy regime. This transforms inference into a resource\-intelligent thermodynamic process that allocates computation where uncertainty reduction is maximized. We present a concrete systems design, pseudocode, and integration plan, demonstrating how entropy can serve as a first\-class control signal for scalable LLM inference.

AI.tldr: They propose an entropy-driven decoding paradigm for LLMs, where inference is controlled by uncertainty instead of token position, integrating scheduling, attention sparsification, and temperature into a single entropy-based controller.
AI.motivation: Current LLM inference engines optimize throughput and latency assuming a fixed, linear token-by-token decoding process, which ignores how uncertainty actually evolves during generation. This can waste compute on low-uncertainty regions and fail to adapt resources where predictions are hard. The authors want a more principled way to allocate computation that corresponds to where the model is most uncertain, improving efficiency and stability at scale.
AI.method: They redefine inference as an entropic-time process, where the primary control variable is the entropy of the model’s predictive distribution. They extend vLLM with: (1) entropy-aware scheduling of decoding steps across requests, (2) entropic pruning of paged attention blocks to sparsify attention based on uncertainty, and (3) adaptive temperature control that dynamically tunes sampling temperature to keep generation near a target entropy band. All three are jointly coordinated under a single entropy-control objective, turning the system into a self-organizing architecture driven by uncertainty flow.
AI.result: They provide a concrete system design, pseudocode, and an integration plan showing that standard vLLM can be augmented so that entropy acts as a first-class control signal. While the abstract does not give quantitative benchmarks, it claims that the resulting system behaves as a resource-intelligent process that directs computation toward regions where it most reduces uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Entropy can be elevated from a passive diagnostic of model predictions to an active control signal governing scheduling, attention allocation, and sampling behavior in LLM inference. Under this entropic-time paradigm, inference becomes a thermodynamics-like process that adaptively allocates limited compute to maximize uncertainty reduction, offering a scalable and principled way to improve efficiency and stability of LLM decoding.

title: The Logovista English--Japanese Machine Translation System
summary:
This paper documents the architecture, development practices, and preserved artifacts of the Logovista English--Japanese machine translation system, a large, explicitly rule-based MT system that was developed and sold commercially from the early 1990s through at least 2012. The system combined hand-authored grammatical rules, a large central dictionary encoding syntactic and semantic constraints, and chart-based parsing with weighted interpretation scoring to manage extensive structural ambiguity.
  The account emphasizes how the system was extended and maintained under real-world usage pressures, including regression control, ambiguity management, and the limits encountered as coverage expanded. Unlike many rule-based MT systems described primarily in research settings, Logovista was deployed for decades and evolved continuously in response to practical requirements. The paper is intended as a technical and historical record rather than an argument for reviving rule-based MT, and describes the software and linguistic resources that have been preserved for potential future study.

AI.tldr: Technical and historical description of the Logovista English–Japanese rule-based MT system, its architecture, evolution, and preserved resources.
AI.motivation: To formally document a long-lived, commercially deployed, rule-based English–Japanese machine translation system (Logovista) whose details and evolution under real-world conditions have not been fully recorded, preserving knowledge that is often lost when such systems are superseded by newer paradigms.
AI.method: Provide an engineering-style account of the system: describe its rule-based architecture (hand-authored grammatical rules, large central dictionary with syntactic/semantic constraints, chart-based parsing, weighted interpretation scoring), and explain development practices such as regression control, ambiguity management, and maintenance strategies as coverage grew. Summarize preserved software components and linguistic resources for future study.
AI.result: The paper reconstructs and documents how Logovista was designed and implemented, how ambiguity and structural complexity were handled via chart parsing and weighted scoring, and how the system was maintained and extended over decades of commercial use. It identifies concrete practices, limitations encountered as coverage expanded, and the specific artifacts (code, rules, dictionaries) that have been preserved.
AI.conclusion: Logovista illustrates both the strengths and limitations of large, hand-built rule-based MT systems in real-world, long-term deployment. While the paper does not advocate returning to rule-based MT, it preserves important technical and historical details, along with surviving software and linguistic resources, to support future research, analysis, or comparative study of MT approaches.

title: Escaping the BLEU Trap: A Signal-Grounded Framework with Decoupled Semantic Guidance for EEG-to-Text Decoding
summary: Decoding natural language from non-invasive EEG signals is a promising yet challenging task. However, current state-of-the-art models remain constrained by three fundamental limitations: Semantic Bias (mode collapse into generic templates), Signal Neglect (hallucination based on linguistic priors rather than neural inputs), and the BLEU Trap, where evaluation metrics are artificially inflated by high-frequency stopwords, masking a lack of true semantic fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose SemKey, a novel multi-stage framework that enforces signal-grounded generation through four decoupled semantic objectives: sentiment, topic, length, and surprisal. We redesign the interaction between the neural encoder and the Large Language Model (LLM) by injecting semantic prompts as Queries and EEG embeddings as Key-Value pairs, strictly forcing the model to attend to neural inputs. Furthermore, we move beyond standard translation metrics by adopting N-way Retrieval Accuracy and Fréchet Distance to rigorously assess diversity and alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively eliminates hallucinations on noise inputs and achieves SOTA performance on these robust protocols. Code will be released upon acceptance at https://github.com/xmed-lab/SemKey.

AI.tldr: SemKey is a new EEG-to-language decoding framework that uses explicit semantic controls and revised evaluation metrics to produce signal-grounded, diverse, and robust text generations from non-invasive brain signals.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG-to-language decoders suffer from semantic bias (generic template outputs), over-reliance on language priors instead of EEG signals (signal neglect), and misleading evaluation via n-gram metrics like BLEU that can look good due to stopwords while missing true semantic alignment. There is a need for models and metrics that ensure generated text is actually driven by neural data and captures its semantic content faithfully and diversely.
AI.method: The authors propose SemKey, a multi-stage framework with four disentangled semantic objectives: sentiment, topic, length, and surprisal. They modify the interaction pattern between an EEG encoder and an LLM so that semantic prompts are injected as Queries, while EEG embeddings are used as Key-Value pairs in attention, structurally forcing the LLM to condition on neural signals. The framework integrates these semantic controls to guide decoding and is trained/evaluated under this constrained attention setup.
AI.result: Experiments show that SemKey effectively avoids hallucinations when presented with noisy EEG inputs and sets new state-of-the-art performance on more robust evaluation schemes. Specifically, it surpasses prior methods under N-way Retrieval Accuracy and Fréchet Distance, metrics that better capture semantic alignment and diversity than BLEU-like scores.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling multiple semantic aspects and redesigning the LLM-EEG interaction so that neural signals must be attended to, SemKey produces more faithful and diverse language decoding from EEG. Combined with stronger evaluation metrics that go beyond BLEU, the framework addresses key shortcomings of previous systems and establishes a more reliable protocol for non-invasive neural language decoding research.

title: How does fine-tuning improve sensorimotor representations in large language models?
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit a significant "embodiment gap", where their text-based representations fail to align with human sensorimotor experiences. This study systematically investigates whether and how task-specific fine-tuning can bridge this gap. Utilizing Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and dimension-specific correlation metrics, we demonstrate that the internal representations of LLMs can be steered toward more embodied, grounded patterns through fine-tuning. Furthermore, the results show that while sensorimotor improvements generalize robustly across languages and related sensory-motor dimensions, they are highly sensitive to the learning objective, failing to transfer across two disparate task formats.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether task-specific fine-tuning can make LLMs’ internal representations more aligned with human sensorimotor experience, and finds that it can, but the gains are sensitive to the exact learning objective.
AI.motivation: LLMs are trained purely on text, so their internal representations often lack grounding in real-world sensorimotor experience, creating an “embodiment gap.” Understanding whether and how this gap can be reduced is important for building more human-like, grounded language systems and for clarifying what kinds of training signals are needed to imbue LLMs with embodied knowledge.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune LLMs on specific tasks designed to encourage sensorimotor grounding and then analyze changes in their internal representations. They use Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and dimension-specific correlation metrics to compare model representations before and after fine-tuning, and to assess how well these representations align with human sensorimotor patterns across languages and sensory-motor dimensions. They also compare different task formats to test transfer across learning objectives.
AI.result: Fine-tuning can shift LLM internal representations toward more embodied, grounded patterns, as quantified by higher alignment in RSA and correlation metrics with human sensorimotor data. These improvements generalize well across languages and across related sensory-motor dimensions, indicating a degree of robustness. However, the benefits are strongly tied to the specific learning objective: improvements achieved in one task format do not transfer reliably to a distinct task format, indicating limited cross-task generalization of embodiment gains.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific fine-tuning is an effective way to reduce the embodiment gap in LLMs, making their internal representations more consistent with human sensorimotor experience. Nonetheless, this grounding is fragile with respect to the training objective: different task formats do not easily share or inherit the learned sensorimotor improvements. This suggests that achieving robust, broadly embodied language understanding may require carefully designed objectives or multi-task training rather than relying on a single fine-tuning task.

title: Towards Self-Robust LLMs: Intrinsic Prompt Noise Resistance via CoIPO
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable and steadily improving performance across a wide range of tasks. However, LLM performance may be highly sensitive to prompt variations especially in scenarios with limited openness or strict output formatting requirements, indicating insufficient robustness. In real-world applications, user prompts provided to LLMs often contain imperfections, which may undermine the quality of the model's responses. To address this issue, previous work has primarily focused on preprocessing prompts, employing external tools or even LLMs to refine prompt formulations in advance. However, these approaches overlook the intrinsic robustness of LLMs, and their reliance on external components introduces additional computational overhead and uncertainty. In this work, we propose a Contrastive Learning-based Inverse Direct Preference Optimization (CoIPO) method that minimizes the discrepancy between the label-aligned logits produced by the model under a clean prompt and its noisy counterpart, and conduct a detailed analysis using mutual information theory. We augment the FLAN dataset by constructing paired prompts, each consisting of a clean prompt and its corresponding noisy version for training. Additionally, to evaluate the effectiveness, we develop NoisyPromptBench, a benchmark enhanced and derived from the existing PromptBench. Experimental results conducted on NoisyPromptBench demonstrate that our proposed method achieves a significant improvement in average accuracy over the current state-of-the-art approaches. The source code of CoIPO, pair-wise FLAN datasets, and NoisyPromptBench have already been released on https://github.com/vegetable-yx/CoIPO.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoIPO, a contrastive learning-based training method to make large language models robust to noisy, imperfect prompts, and evaluates it with a new benchmark, NoisyPromptBench.
AI.motivation: LLMs are sensitive to variations and imperfections in user prompts, especially in constrained or low-openness settings, which harms reliability in real applications. Existing solutions mostly preprocess or rewrite prompts using external tools or other LLMs, adding cost and ignoring the model’s inherent robustness. The authors want a training-time method that directly improves robustness of LLMs to noisy prompts without heavy external machinery.
AI.method: They propose Contrastive Learning-based Inverse Direct Preference Optimization (CoIPO). During training, they construct paired prompts from the FLAN dataset: each pair has a clean prompt and a corresponding noisy version. CoIPO minimizes the discrepancy between the label-aligned logits of the model when given the clean prompt versus the noisy prompt, framed via contrastive learning and analyzed with mutual information theory. This aligns model behavior across prompt variants so that noisy prompts elicit predictions similar to those from clean prompts.
AI.result: They build NoisyPromptBench, an evaluation benchmark derived from PromptBench that systematically tests models under noisy prompts. Experiments on NoisyPromptBench show that models trained with CoIPO achieve substantially higher average accuracy compared with current state-of-the-art robustness approaches under noisy-prompt conditions.
AI.conclusion: CoIPO effectively enhances LLM robustness to noisy or imperfect prompts by aligning model responses across clean–noisy prompt pairs at the logit level, without relying on external prompt-preprocessing tools. The new NoisyPromptBench benchmark and released code/datasets support future research on prompt-robust LLMs.

title: M-QUEST -- Meme Question-Understanding Evaluation on Semantics and Toxicity
summary: Internet memes are a powerful form of online communication, yet their nature and reliance on commonsense knowledge make toxicity detection challenging. Identifying key features for meme interpretation and understanding, is a crucial task. Previous work has been focused on some elements contributing to the meaning, such as the Textual dimension via OCR, the Visual dimension via object recognition, upper layers of meaning like the Emotional dimension, Toxicity detection via proxy variables, such as hate speech detection, and sentiment analysis. Nevertheless, there is still a lack of an overall architecture able to formally identify elements contributing to the meaning of a meme, and be used in the sense-making process. In this work, we present a semantic framework and a corresponding benchmark for automatic knowledge extraction from memes. First, we identify the necessary dimensions to understand and interpret a meme: Textual material, Visual material, Scene, Background Knowledge, Emotion, Semiotic Projection, Analogical Mapping, Overall Intent, Target Community, and Toxicity Assessment. Second, the framework guides a semi-automatic process of generating a benchmark with commonsense question-answer pairs about meme toxicity assessment and its underlying reason. The resulting benchmark M-QUEST consists of 609 question-answer pairs for 307 memes. Thirdly, we evaluate eight open-source large language models on their ability to correctly solve M-QUEST. Our results show that current models' commonsense reasoning capabilities for toxic meme interpretation vary depending on the dimension and architecture. Models with instruction tuning and reasoning capabilities significantly outperform the others, though pragmatic inference questions remain challenging. We release code, benchmark, and prompts to support future research intersecting multimodal content safety and commonsense reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a semantic framework and benchmark (M-QUEST) for commonsense-based toxicity understanding in Internet memes, then evaluates multiple open-source LLMs on it.
AI.motivation: Internet memes are hard to analyze for toxicity because they combine text, images, and implicit commonsense knowledge. Existing work focuses on isolated aspects (text OCR, object recognition, sentiment, hate speech) but lacks a unified, formal architecture that captures all the semantic dimensions needed to interpret a meme and assess its toxicity. There is also no standardized benchmark targeting commonsense reasoning about why a meme is or isn’t toxic.
AI.method: The authors define a semantic framework listing key dimensions required for meme understanding: Textual material, Visual material, Scene, Background Knowledge, Emotion, Semiotic Projection, Analogical Mapping, Overall Intent, Target Community, and Toxicity Assessment. Using this framework, they design a semi-automatic pipeline to build a benchmark of commonsense question–answer pairs about memes and their toxicity. They compile M-QUEST: 609 QA pairs over 307 memes. They then test eight open-source LLMs on this benchmark to measure their performance across dimensions, focusing on how instruction tuning and reasoning-oriented architectures impact results.
AI.result: M-QUEST is produced as a labeled benchmark that covers 10 semantic dimensions of meme interpretation and toxicity assessment. Experimental evaluation shows substantial variation among models: those that are instruction-tuned and explicitly geared toward reasoning perform notably better than other open-source models. However, all models still struggle with pragmatic inference questions, indicating that current commonsense reasoning for nuanced toxic meme understanding is limited.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semantic framework provides a structured way to decompose meme meaning into multiple dimensions and to guide benchmark construction. M-QUEST offers a new resource to evaluate LLMs’ commonsense reasoning for toxic meme interpretation. While some instruction-tuned, reasoning-focused models outperform others, pragmatic and subtle aspects of meme toxicity remain difficult, underscoring the need for improved multimodal content safety methods and better commonsense reasoning capabilities. The authors release their code, benchmark, and prompts to foster further research in this area.

title: The Influence of Iconicity in Transfer Learning for Sign Language Recognition
summary: Most sign language recognition research relies on Transfer Learning (TL) from vision-based datasets such as ImageNet. Some extend this to alternatively available language datasets, often focusing on signs with cross-linguistic similarities. This body of work examines the necessity of these likenesses on effective knowledge transfer by comparing TL performance between iconic signs of two different sign language pairs: Chinese to Arabic and Greek to Flemish. Google Mediapipe was utilised as an input feature extractor, enabling spatial information of these signs to be processed with a Multilayer Perceptron architecture and the temporal information with a Gated Recurrent Unit. Experimental results showed a 7.02% improvement for Arabic and 1.07% for Flemish when conducting iconic TL from Chinese and Greek respectively.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether visual-iconic similarity between signs in different sign languages is necessary for effective transfer learning and finds modest but measurable gains when transferring between iconic sign pairs.
AI.motivation: Most sign language recognition systems rely on transfer learning from large, generic, vision-based datasets like ImageNet, which do not capture linguistic and iconic properties of sign languages. Some recent work instead transfers from other sign-language or gesture datasets, assuming that cross-linguistic iconic similarities between signs help. However, it is unclear how necessary or beneficial such iconic similarity actually is, and whether it consistently improves recognition performance across different language pairs.
AI.method: The authors study transfer learning between pairs of sign languages that share iconic similarities in specific signs: Chinese to Arabic, and Greek to Flemish. They use Google Mediapipe to extract spatial keypoint features from video, then process spatial information using a Multilayer Perceptron and temporal (sequential) dynamics using a Gated Recurrent Unit network. They compare recognition performance with and without doing transfer learning based on iconic sign correspondences for each language pair.
AI.result: Transfer learning based on iconic sign correspondences improved recognition accuracy in both considered language pairs. For the Chinese-to-Arabic transfer, performance increased by 7.02%, and for the Greek-to-Flemish transfer, performance increased by 1.07% compared to the corresponding non-iconic or baseline setups.
AI.conclusion: Iconic similarity between signs across different sign languages can provide measurable benefits to transfer learning in sign recognition models, although the magnitude of improvement varies between language pairs. This suggests that leveraging cross-linguistic iconic correspondences is a promising but not uniformly strong signal and should be considered as one factor among others in designing sign language recognition transfer learning pipelines.

title: Retcon -- a Prompt-Based Technique for Precise Control of LLMs in Conversations
comment: 5 pages, 2 figures, 3 appendixes with prompts and examples
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) allow agents to execute complex natural language tasks. Many LLM applications, such as support agents, teaching assistants, and interactive bots, involve multi-turn conversations. However, it remains challenging to control LLMs in the context of such interactions, particularly when the LLM behavior needs to be adjustable over the course of the conversation. In this paper, we present Retcon, a few-shot prompting technique designed to provide turn-level control over LLMs in conversations. We then demonstrate that it performs significantly better than zero-shot and traditional few-shot prompting.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Retcon, a few-shot prompting method that gives fine-grained, per-turn control over LLM behavior in multi-turn conversations and outperforms standard prompting baselines.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents increasingly operate in multi-turn conversational settings (e.g., support agents, teaching assistants, chatbots), where their behavior may need to change over the course of an interaction. Existing prompting approaches (zero-shot or standard few-shot) provide limited and coarse control, making it hard to dynamically steer the model at the level of individual turns.
AI.method: The authors propose Retcon, a specialized few-shot prompting technique tailored to conversational use. Retcon structures the prompt to include examples that demonstrate how the model should behave at each turn of a dialogue, enabling explicit, turn-level control. This contrasts with conventional few-shot prompts that typically define behavior only at a global level. The paper empirically compares Retcon against zero-shot and traditional few-shot prompting on conversational tasks requiring behavior control across turns.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows that Retcon achieves significantly better performance than both zero-shot and standard few-shot prompting when used to control LLM behavior in multi-turn conversations. The improvement is specifically notable in settings where conversational behavior must change or be adjusted across turns.
AI.conclusion: Turn-level control is crucial for practical conversational LLM applications, and Retcon is an effective and simple prompting-based solution. The technique outperforms common baselines, indicating that careful prompt design focused on per-turn behavior can substantially improve controllability in interactive LLM systems.

title: Quantum-Inspired Self-Attention in a Large Language Model
summary: Recent advances in Natural Language Processing have been predominantly driven by transformer-based architectures, which rely heavily on self-attention mechanisms to model relationships between tokens in a sequence. Similarly, the field of Quantum Natural Language Processing, which seeks to leverage quantum principles to address challenges in language understanding and generation tasks, has seen the recent development of quantum self-attention mechanisms. We propose a classical quantum-inspired self-attention (QISA) mechanism and integrate it into the full autoregressive language modeling pipeline of GPT-1. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first integration of this kind, as previous quantum self-attention mechanisms have been primarily tested on text classification. In our experiments, QISA achieves better performance when compared to standard self-attention on the metrics character error rate ($15.5\times$ better), word error rate ($4.7 \times $) and cross-entropy loss ($13 \times$). This is achieved while only requiring a $ 2.6\times$ longer inference time.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quantum‑inspired self‑attention mechanism (QISA) and plugs it into a GPT‑1 style language model, achieving notably better error and loss metrics than standard self‑attention at the cost of slower inference.
AI.motivation: Transformer self‑attention is central to modern NLP, and quantum NLP is exploring quantum self‑attention, but existing quantum approaches are mostly confined to classification tasks and run on quantum hardware. There is a gap in understanding whether quantum‑inspired ideas can improve full autoregressive language modeling on classical hardware, and whether they can outperform standard self‑attention in practical metrics such as error rates and loss.
AI.method: Design a classical, quantum‑inspired self‑attention mechanism (QISA) that borrows principles from quantum self‑attention, then replace the standard self‑attention blocks in a GPT‑1‑style autoregressive language model with QISA. Train and evaluate this modified model on a language modeling task, comparing its performance against a baseline GPT‑1 with standard self‑attention using character error rate, word error rate, cross‑entropy loss, and inference time.
AI.result: The model with QISA self‑attention outperforms the standard self‑attention baseline: character error rate improves by a factor of 15.5, word error rate by 4.7, and cross‑entropy loss by 13, while inference becomes 2.6 times slower than the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Quantum‑inspired self‑attention can be integrated into a full autoregressive transformer language model and can substantially improve language modeling quality metrics compared with standard self‑attention, though at the cost of increased inference time. This suggests that quantum‑inspired mechanisms are a promising direction for enhancing classical NLP models and merit further optimization and study.

title: Automated Concept Discovery for LLM-as-a-Judge Preference Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as scalable evaluators of model outputs, but their preference judgments exhibit systematic biases and can diverge from human evaluations. Prior work on LLM-as-a-judge has largely focused on a small, predefined set of hypothesized biases, leaving open the problem of automatically discovering unknown drivers of LLM preferences. We address this gap by studying several embedding-level concept extraction methods for analyzing LLM judge behavior. We compare these methods in terms of interpretability and predictiveness, finding that sparse autoencoder-based approaches recover substantially more interpretable preference features than alternatives while remaining competitive in predicting LLM decisions. Using over 27k paired responses from multiple human preference datasets and judgments from three LLMs, we analyze LLM judgments and compare them to those of human annotators.
